root_goal: how to visualize the data of air pollution

args.query_intent: how to visualize the data of air pollution

orchestrator_plan.reason: 使用用户原始问题作为初始检索条件，获取相关文献基础
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 本策略旨在满足用户希望“可视化空气污染数据”的需求，围绕如何把浓度、分布和变化信息以图形方式呈现进行检索。结果主要聚焦在各类可视化图形——包括隧道内多种污染物浓度随距离变化的曲线图、机动车道上下风向的垂直剖面图、基于 MIX Asian 排放清单的 0.25°×0.25° 网格地图、展示 AQI、PM₂.₅、SO₂、NO₂ 等指标在不同区域比例的柱状图、以及关联空气质量变化与社会经济因素的相关系数热图等。多数条目不仅提供了图形本身，还伴随具体的定量描述（如浓度增长率、线性相关系数 R²≈0.96、区域百分比分布），并说明使用的可视化工具或软件（ArcGIS、GeoDa、ELPI 等），从而为进一步的图表制作、空间分析或模型构建提供了实证素材。这些内容共同揭示了空气污染的数据可视化可以通过浓度剖面、网格分布、条形统计和关联热图等多维度手段实现，为用户后续实现交互式或出版级图形提供了丰富且可直接复用的参考线索。

content.text: Visual representation of concentration profiles for multiple pollutants measured at varying distances within a tunnel, showing trends of accumulation and mixing. The figure demonstrates an increasing concentration trend for pollutants, indicating emissions accumulation and air mixing patterns in the tunnel.

The black solid line represents the average of 12 repeated measurements, and the gray shaded area represents the standard deviation.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Mobile measurement of vehicle emission factors in\\figure_2.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["CO2", "SO2", "NO", "NO2", "O3", "SVOCs", "PM10", "PM2.5"], "concise_summary": "Visual representation of concentration profiles for multiple pollutants measured at varying distances within a tunnel, showing trends of accumulation and mixing.", "inferred_insight": "The figure demonstrates an increasing concentration trend for pollutants, indicating emissions accumulation and air mixing patterns in the tunnel.", "references": ["All target pollutants, except  $\\mathrm{O_3}$ , displayed an increasing trend from the entrance to the exit of the tunnel (Fig. 2). According to the  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}, \\mathrm{SO}_{2}$  and  $\\mathrm{NO_X}$  results, a zonation of the tunnel could be established: mixing zone (entrance to  $600\\mathrm{m}$ ) and accumulation zone ( $600\\mathrm{m}$  to exit). The  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  concentration ranged from 457 to  $823~\\mathrm{ppm}$ , with a slow increase from the entrance to about  $600\\mathrm{m}$  (rate of increase:  $27.6~\\mathrm{ppm~km}^{-1}$ ) and a rapid increase from  $600\\mathrm{m}$  to the exit (rate of increase:  $207~\\mathrm{ppm~km}^{-1}$ ). The  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  profile reflected an accumulation of vehicle emissions in the tunnel (Gamnitzer et al., 2006), and indicated the physical mixing of the external air inflow with vehicle exhaust until around  $600\\mathrm{m}$  from the entrance (Fig. 2a). The  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  concentration ranged from 3.06 to  $53.2~\\mathrm{ppb}$  and the rate of increase in the mixing and accumulation zone was 5.42"], "chunk_type": "figure"}

source_args.query_intent: how to visualize the data of air pollution

evaluation.extracted_insight: Figure 2 shows concentration profiles of CO2, SO2, NO, NO2, O3, PM10 and PM2.5 along a road tunnel; CO2 rises from 457 to 823 ppm with a slow increase (27.6 ppm km⁻¹) up to 600 m (mixing zone) and a rapid increase (207 ppm km⁻¹) thereafter (accumulation zone), indicating vehicle emission build‑up and mixing of external air.
evaluation.reason: Contains quantitative concentration trends and explicit rate values; metadata includes paper ID and figure ID, enabling deeper analysis of emission accumulation and zone definition.

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

{"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_165", "figure_title": "Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "Guan, D., Liu, Z., Geng, Y., Lindner, S., and Hubacek, K.: The gigatonne gap in China's carbon dioxide inventories, Nature Clim. content.text: Vertical profiles show particle concentrations upwind and downwind of a motorway, revealing height-dependent differences and correlations with NOx levels. metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Comparison of measured and model-calculated real-w", "figure_id": "img_56", "figure_title": "Fig. 5. Vertical profiles of mean particle size concentrations measured by the ELPI instrument on the upwind side (13 May, 23:00-24:00 CEST) as well as on the downwind side around noon (14 May, 11:00-14:30 CEST).", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Comparison of measured and model-calculated real-w\\figure_5.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Heatmap"]}, "key_entities": ["ELPI instrument", "particle size concentrations", "upwind side", "downwind side", "plume", "motorway", "soot particles", "nucleation mode particles", "tyre and brake abrasion", "resuspension", "particle number concentrations", "NOx concentrations", "emission factors", "LDV", "HDV"], "concise_summary": "Vertical profiles show particle concentrations upwind and downwind of a motorway, revealing height-dependent differences and correlations with NOx levels.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates how particle concentrations vary with height and time, confirming correlations with NOx and revealing impacts of emissions on air quality.", "references": ["For the first time, vertical profiles with high temporal and spatial resolution of particulate air pollutants could be recorded by tower elevators (Imhof et al., 2005). Upwind the motorway, no height-dependent difference in the particle number and surface area concentration was observed. On the downwind side, substantially higher concentrations were measured within the plume from near the ground up to  $20\\mathrm{m}$  height than above the plume (Fig. 5). This height dependency was found for particles with diameter  $< 300\\mathrm{nm}$ , which are soot particles and nucleation mode particles formed by condensation of exhaust gases in most cases and for coarse particles (diameter  $>1\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ), originating from tyre and brake abrasion and resuspension of soil dust. Between 300 and  $700\\mathrm{nm}$  particle size, no height dependence was found, because motor vehicles emit only a few particles in this size range.", "After adjusting the instruments for their different time lags, the vertical distribution of the particle number concentrations showed a similar behaviour as the  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  concentrations: No height dependence on the upwind side and a continuous decrease with increasing height from 5 to  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above the earth's surface on the downwind side was found. Consequently, the results in this study confirmed the correlation between particles and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  values that had also been observed in former case studies. A linear relation between the particle surface area (SA) and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  with correlation coefficients of  $R^2 = 0.96$  and 0.89 for 2 downwind cases was found. At around  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above ground, the background value of SA was attained. By subtracting the background value from the concentrations measured in the near-surface air layer, direct emissions of the road were obtained, even if contemporaneous upwind and downwind measurements were missing. Together with the parallel wind measurements, mean absolute emission factors (EF) were determined. The PM emissions in the range between  $30\\mathrm{nm}$  and  $10\\mu \\mathrm{m}$  from all vehicles passing the site were found to be  $1.80\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ . Separation into the categories of LDV led to  $1.22\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.03\\mathrm{cm}^3\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  aerosol volume, while the values of HDV were  $7.79\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.41\\mathrm{cm}^3\\mathrm{km}^{-1}$  veh $^{-1}$  particle volume.", "Fig. 4. Vertical profiles of mean particle size distributions measured by SMPS instruments at three different heights on the upwind side of the motorway (13 May 2001, midnight) and on the downwind side (14 May 2001, morning rush hour and noon)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

source_args.query_intent: how to visualize the data of air pollution

evaluation.extracted_insight: Vertical profiles from ELPI measurements show that downwind of a motorway particle number and surface‑area concentrations are markedly higher up to 20 m height, with a strong linear correlation to NOx (R² = 0.96); these profiles enable derivation of emission factors for light‑duty (LDV) and heavy‑duty vehicles (HDV).
evaluation.reason: Provides quantitative vertical distribution data and a clear statistical relationship between particles and NOx, directly supporting emission‑factor estimation for traffic sources.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden", "figure_id": "img_71", "figure_title": "Fig. 4. Estimated coefficients (date) prior to and following the start time of heating.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\\figure_4.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "PM2.5", "SO2", "NO2", "Eastern region", "Western region", "WCHP project"], "concise_summary": "The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.", "references": ["As Fig. 4 shows, the 15 day estimator prior to and following the start of heating varied around a value of zero (0) devoid of a clear trend, and most coefficients were not statistically significant. This result indicated a similarity between pilot cities and non-pilot cities regarding AQI. Despite an abnormal increase 4-6 days following the start time of heating, such differences decreased to a negative value (as seen 2 days prior to the start time of heating), and this trend maintained for the ensuing two weeks. These results were consistent with the hypothesis that the WCHP project generated differential trends in pollutant concentrations between pilot and non-pilot cities. It is noteworthy that the period prior to the start time of heating was implemented and continued for 3-4 days in some cities prior to the formal start time of heating, which may have resulted in the early decrease. Additionally, we also estimated the Heatperiod coefficient 30 days following the start time of heating, which also indicated that effects of the WCHP project existed following the start time of heating.", "where  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  is the set of dummy variables, which represents whether the  $i$ -th city is in a heating period at  $n-1$ -day.  $L_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days prior to the start time of heating, and  $E_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days following the start time of heating. With the omitted time category being the start time of heating,  $L_n$  captures the differences in pollutant indicators between pilot and non-pilot cities relative to differences just prior to the launch of the policy. Here we selected AQI as the air pollutant proxy. Under such specifications, if the signs of the estimated  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  coefficients differ, it can be assumed that changes in pollutant concentrations are not significant.", "As a second robustness check, we constructed a counterfactual test to check whether our results could be driven by other unobserved factors. In doing so, we selected a different date during the non-heating period (May, June, July, August, September, and October) in lieu of the real start date of heating, specifying the interaction term between the treatment variable and the pseudoheating start time variable as Pseudo-HCP. The WCHP project can only take effect during the heating period, while the pseudo-HCP coefficient can reflect the impact of the WCHP project during the non-heating period. If estimated coefficients of pseudo-WCHP are significant and have the same sign as of the true WCHP shown in Table 4, we can conclude that air quality improvements in pilot cities could not be mainly attributed to the WCHP project, and the robustness of the DID model would be inadequate. In this case, improvements may be related to other pollution control policies, such as production restrictions and limits to the number of private vehicle allowed on roads.", "According to the regressions shown in Table 5, only when the start time of heating was specified in October, coefficients of pseudo-WCHP were significantly negative at a  $1\\%$  level, while other estimation results were not significant or had a positive sign. Compared to Table 4, these conflicting results failed to reject the validity of the \"WHCD\" policy. That is to say, the effect of the WCHP project was evident. In fact, we specified the heating start time on the 15th of November in consideration of the \"official\" start time of central heating while ignoring differences caused by scattered coal combustion. In October, some rural areas in the pilot and non-pilot cities began to burn scattered coal for heating, which could cause an evident increase in pollution emissions [47,48]."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: To further analyze the temporal and spatial variation features of air quality in 31 provincial capital cities from 2013 to 2020, the spatial autocorrelation analysis method is applied. ArcGIS 10.6 and GeoDa software is used for the calculation and analytic process.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont", "figure_id": "img_42", "figure_title": "Fig. 1. Map of the study domain and definition of urban (red) and rural (gray) regions over the CONUS. The yellow triangle symbols represent the location of ozonesonde stations (BLD: Boulder, HVA: Huntsville). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont\\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup", "Describe Data Distribution"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["CONUS", "ozonesonde stations", "CMAQ", "WRF", "urban regions", "rural regions", "Boulder", "Huntsville"], "concise_summary": "The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville).", "inferred_insight": "Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.", "references": ["To compute both atmospheric concentrations of gaseous and particulate pollutants and first-order sensitivity coefficients, required for conducting inverse modeling, we use the Community Multiscale Air Quality model configured with the decoupled direct method in three dimensions (CMAQ DDM-3D) based on CMAQ model version 5.2 (Cohan et al., 2005; Napelenok et al., 2006), developed and released by the EPA. Our study domain covering the CONUS utilizes  $12\\mathrm{km}$  grid spacing with  $459\\times 299$  grid points (shown in Fig. 1), while the vertical extent between the surface and  $100\\mathrm{hPa}$  is discretized with 27 layers of variable thickness. We use carbon bond (CB6r3) for the gas phase and AERO6 for the aerosol chemical mechanism. We obtained the chemical initial and boundary conditions from the CMAQ model with the in-line dust model covering the entire Northern Hemisphere (HCMAQ) (Mathur et al., 2017), providing dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions in a consistent physical configuration. Details of the HCMAQ simulation and its emission data can be found in Jung et al. (2021) and Vukovich et al. (2018). Since the HCMAQ simulation was based on the year 2016, we applied seasonal scaling for gaseous species based on the comparison", "Using the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model version 4.0, we estimated meteorological fields over the U.S.  $12\\mathrm{km}$  modeling domain (shown in Fig. 1) with the initial and boundary conditions from the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR), which has a  $32\\mathrm{km}$  horizontal resolution and  $3\\mathrm{h}$  of temporal resolution. Detailed configurations of both the CMAQ and WRF models can be found in Table 1. In an effort to enhance model performance in simulating meteorological fields, we applied indirect soil moisture and temperature nudging technique (Pleim and Gilliam, 2009; Pleim and Xiu, 2003) together with a four-", "The variability of satellite-observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns reflects changes in bottom-up emissions and the contribution of background sources in the upper troposphere (Silvern et al., 2019). In Fig. 3, we plotted seasonal averaged changes in observed and simulated tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities in four regions: domainwide (i.e., CONUS), urban and rural regions, and southeastern (SE) areas. We defined an urban area based on the U.S. census-based surrogate, where population density is at least 1000 people per square mile, denoted in red in Fig. 1; and we defined a rural region, designated by gray in Fig. 1, as more than  $20\\mathrm{km}$  away from both urban regions and power plants with a capacity of 1 MW or more, based on the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The southeastern (SE) area (a blue rectangle shown in Fig. 1) denotes an area with dominant lightning-induced (to the total  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions) and biogenic emissions (to the total NMVOCs emissions) over the CONUS. In SE, NMVOCs emissions from biogenic sources are dominant, so we excluded the SE area when we analyzed modeled and observed HCHO columns over urban and rural regions in Fig. 3.", "Fig. 3. The long-term trend in summertime averaged tropospheric (a-1 to h-1)  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and (a-2 to h-2) HCHO columns observed by the OMI satellite (2011-2018) and simulated by the CMAQ model (2011, 2014, and 2017), and their relative changes in the CONUS and urban, rural, and southeastern U.S. regions (shown in Fig. 1). CMAQ simulations were conducted with the prior emission and three background setups (with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and lightning emissions (LNO), with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions, and with profile lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

**检索过程总结** **1. 用户需求** 用户希望了解 **“如何可视化空气污染数据”**，即寻找能够直观呈现污染物浓度、空间分布及时间变化的图表、地图或交互式工具。 **2. 检索策略** 采用 **semantic‑search**（语义检索）并将用户原始问题直接作为查询意图，旨在捕获与空气污染可视化相关的学术文献、案例图示和技术方法。 **3. 检索结果概览** - 总计检索到 **10 条** 相关文献/图示。 - 按价值划分：**高价值 4 条、 中等价值 4 条、 低价值 2 条**。 | 价值层级 | 关键内容 | 评估 | |----------|----------|------| | 高价值 | • 图 2：道路隧道内 CO₂、SO₂、NO、NO₂、O₃、PM10、PM2.5 的浓度剖面，展示了浓度随距离的线性和快速上升阶段。
• 图 12：0.25°×0.25° 网格的亚洲十种气体与气溶胶排放图，揭示了区域排放模式。 | GROW / KEEP | | 中等价值 | • 柱状图：AQI、PM2.5、SO₂、NO₂ 在全样本及东西部区域的分布比例，突出区域差异。
• 图 1：美国大陆研究域地图，标明城市/农村分区及平流层探测站位置，为后续臭氧‑化学分析提供空间框架。 | KEEP | | 低价值 | • 数据集说明：公开的气象与空气质量数据来源（中国气象数据服务），对可视化本身无直接示例。 | PRUNE | **4. 评估分析** - **相关性**：大多数文献直接展示了污染物的空间或时间剖面，符合“可视化”需求。 - **权威性**：文献来源于公开的科研项目或权威排放清单（如 MIX Asian emission inventory），可信度较高。 - **完整性**：高价值结果提供了从单点浓度曲线到大尺度网格图的多层次视角，覆盖了常见的可视化形式（折线图、柱状图、热力图、网格地图）。 - **实用性**：图表搭配简要解读，帮助用户快速把握不同可视化手段的适用场景与解读要点。 **5. 关键洞察** 1. **浓度剖面图**（如图 2）非常适合展示特定线路（道路、隧道）内污染物随距离的变化，能直观区分混合区与积累区。 2. **网格热力图**（如图 12）是展示大范围（国家/大陆）排放空间分布的首选，适合对比不同地区的排放强度。 3. **柱状/堆叠柱状图**能够突出区域间（如东西部）的污染水平差异，适用于政策评估或项目影响分析。 4. **基础地图结合站点标注**（如图 1）为后续时间序列或模式分析提供地理框架，便于将监测数据与空间特征关联。 **6. 可视化建议** - **小尺度**（单线路/单城市）：使用折线或面积图呈现浓度随距离/时间的连续变化。 - **中尺度**（省/州）：采用分层柱状图或箱线图比较不同地区的主要指标（PM2.5、NO₂ 等）。 - **大尺度**（国家/跨国）：利用等间距网格的热力图或等值线图展示排放或浓度的空间格局。 - **交互式**：结合地图平台（如 Leaflet、Kepler.gl）加入滑块、过滤器，实现多变量、多时间段的动态探索。 **7. 词云数据（供可视化）** 高频关键词（按出现次数排序）：`concentration`, `profile`, `grid map`, `pollutant`, `PM2.5`, `SO2`, `NO2`, `heatmap`, `regional disparity`, `emission inventory`. 这些词可生成词云，快速呈现关注焦点，帮助用户在海量文献中捕捉核心概念。 --- *本总结以简洁流畅的语言概括了检索过程、结果质量及关键可视化洞察，帮助用户快速把握如何选择和实现空气污染数据的有效可视化方案。*

orchestrator_plan.reason: 现有文献主要展示静态图表，缺乏交互式仪表盘的实现细节。检索交互式可视化方案可为用户提供动态探索污染数据的工具，直接对应‘如何可视化’的问题，且在当前证据中未出现。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 这个策略的出发点是想找到能够用 Python 的 Plotly + Dash 构建交互式空气质量可视化仪表盘的实现细节，直接回应用户“如何可视化空气污染数据”的需求。但检索结果几乎全部是传统的科研图表和模型说明：有展示隧道内多污染物浓度随距离变化的趋势图、VOC 源配比的散点回归图、不同地区与时间尺度上 AQI 与主要污染物分布的柱状图、以及基于 ArcGIS / GeoDa 的空间自相关分析流程图和模型域的示意图。虽然这些图像提供了浓度剖面、相关系数、空间分布等实用的可视化思路，但没有出现任何关于 Plotly Dash、交互式控件或仪表盘代码实现的内容。唯一可能的线索是对数据结构和空间‑时间特征的描述（如垂直剖面、区域差异、时序自动相关），这些可以作为后续构建交互式可视化时的数据准备和图层设计的参考，但仍需自行补充交互框架的实现方案。

content.text: The figure displays scatter plots comparing source profiles from measured and initial VOC levels. Linear regressions and confidence intervals are shown, highlighting variability and correlations in source profiles. The figure illustrates strong correlations for solvent usage and gasoline evaporation, but discrepancies in vehicle emissions suggest variability in photochemical processes impacting VOC profiles.

The black solid lines represent the 1:1 line.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds\\figure_8.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Scatter Plot"]}, "key_entities": ["source profiles", "VOC levels", "PMF model", "gasoline evaporation", "solvent usage", "combustion sources", "industrial sources"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays scatter plots comparing source profiles from measured and initial VOC levels. Linear regressions and confidence intervals are shown, highlighting variability and correlations in source profiles.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates strong correlations for solvent usage and gasoline evaporation, but discrepancies in vehicle emissions suggest variability in photochemical processes impacting VOC profiles.", "references": ["results by the PMF modelling. The measured and initial datasets were both introduced into the PMF model and the source profiles (Fig. 4 and Fig. S3) from two datasets were compared as shown in Fig. 8. The source profiles of gasoline evaporation, solvent usage, combustion sources, and industrial sources derived from two different datasets were in line with each other, with Pearson coefficient  $(r^2)$  of 0.99, 0.85, 0.68, and 0.40, respectively. However, a great discrepancy was found for vehicle emissions. The PMF algorithms aim to find the profiles and contributions for a given number of factors that have the minimum weighed residues of the input dataset. If the importance of atmospheric photochemical processes exceeds the differences of emission profiles from various sources, the factors in PMF can still be identified according to the different degrees of photochemical processing (Yuan et al., 2012). The source profiles derived from the two datasets suggested that the importance of photochemistry in the atmosphere exceeded the differences in the VOC compositions of vehicle emissions. Compared to the source apportionment from the measured dataset, the contributions of solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, and industrial sources to total VOC mixing ratio derived from the initial dataset were higher, while vehicle emissions and combustion sources were lower (Fig. S4). The differences suggested that the VOC decay should be considered before source apportionment using the receptor model."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

source_args.query_intent: interactive air quality visualization dashboard Python plotly dash

evaluation.extracted_insight: Scatter plots reveal very high linear correlation for gasoline evaporation (r²≈0.99) and solvent usage (r²≈0.85), moderate for combustion sources (r²≈0.68) and low for industrial sources (r²≈0.40). Vehicle‑emission source profiles deviate strongly, suggesting that atmospheric photochemistry alters VOC composition of vehicle exhaust more than emission‑source differences.
evaluation.reason: The figure supplies quantitative validation of PMF‑derived source profiles and highlights a specific inconsistency (vehicle emissions) that can drive deeper investigation into photochemical processing.

content.text: Vertical profiles show particle concentrations upwind and downwind of a motorway, revealing height-dependent differences and correlations with NOx levels. The figure illustrates how particle concentrations vary with height and time, confirming correlations with NOx and revealing impacts of emissions on air quality.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Comparison of measured and model-calculated real-w", "figure_id": "img_56", "figure_title": "Fig. 5. Upwind the motorway, no height-dependent difference in the particle number and surface area concentration was observed. On the downwind side, substantially higher concentrations were measured within the plume from near the ground up to  $20\\mathrm{m}$  height than above the plume (Fig. 5). This height dependency was found for particles with diameter  $< 300\\mathrm{nm}$ , which are soot particles and nucleation mode particles formed by condensation of exhaust gases in most cases and for coarse particles (diameter  $>1\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ), originating from tyre and brake abrasion and resuspension of soil dust. Between 300 and  $700\\mathrm{nm}$  particle size, no height dependence was found, because motor vehicles emit only a few particles in this size range.", "After adjusting the instruments for their different time lags, the vertical distribution of the particle number concentrations showed a similar behaviour as the  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  concentrations: No height dependence on the upwind side and a continuous decrease with increasing height from 5 to  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above the earth's surface on the downwind side was found. Consequently, the results in this study confirmed the correlation between particles and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  values that had also been observed in former case studies. A linear relation between the particle surface area (SA) and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  with correlation coefficients of  $R^2 = 0.96$  and 0.89 for 2 downwind cases was found. At around  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above ground, the background value of SA was attained. By subtracting the background value from the concentrations measured in the near-surface air layer, direct emissions of the road were obtained, even if contemporaneous upwind and downwind measurements were missing. Together with the parallel wind measurements, mean absolute emission factors (EF) were determined. The PM emissions in the range between  $30\\mathrm{nm}$  and  $10\\mu \\mathrm{m}$  from all vehicles passing the site were found to be  $1.80\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ . Separation into the categories of LDV led to  $1.22\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.03\\mathrm{cm}^3\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  aerosol volume, while the values of HDV were  $7.79\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.41\\mathrm{cm}^3\\mathrm{km}^{-1}$  veh $^{-1}$  particle volume.", "Fig. 4. content.text: background sources in the upper troposphere, while we included open fire and soil emissions in prior emissions, as addressed in Section 2.3. Also, surface meteorological variables (e.g., solar radiation, air temperature, and wind fields) vary concentrations of  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO by altering photolysis rates, lifetime, and dispersion (Goldberg et al., 2020), as well as rates of biogenic isoprene emissions (Guenther et al., 2012). Therefore, the model performance of the meteorological variable simulation is critically linked to the reliability of both concentrations of air pollutants and the estimates of top-down emissions. We evaluated modeled hourly air temperatures and wind fields against the U.S. EPA Air Quality System (AQS) measurements over the CONUS, listed in Table 2. We found that our model and the observations were comparable, showing a strong correspondence (air temperature: IOA > 0.89, wind fields: IOA > 0.61).

The yellow triangle symbols represent the location of ozonesonde stations (BLD: Boulder, HVA: Huntsville). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont\\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup", "Describe Data Distribution"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["CONUS", "ozonesonde stations", "CMAQ", "WRF", "urban regions", "rural regions", "Boulder", "Huntsville"], "concise_summary": "The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville).", "inferred_insight": "Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.", "references": ["To compute both atmospheric concentrations of gaseous and particulate pollutants and first-order sensitivity coefficients, required for conducting inverse modeling, we use the Community Multiscale Air Quality model configured with the decoupled direct method in three dimensions (CMAQ DDM-3D) based on CMAQ model version 5.2 (Cohan et al., 2005; Napelenok et al., 2006), developed and released by the EPA. Our study domain covering the CONUS utilizes  $12\\mathrm{km}$  grid spacing with  $459\\times 299$  grid points (shown in Fig. 1), while the vertical extent between the surface and  $100\\mathrm{hPa}$  is discretized with 27 layers of variable thickness. We use carbon bond (CB6r3) for the gas phase and AERO6 for the aerosol chemical mechanism. We obtained the chemical initial and boundary conditions from the CMAQ model with the in-line dust model covering the entire Northern Hemisphere (HCMAQ) (Mathur et al., 2017), providing dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions in a consistent physical configuration. Details of the HCMAQ simulation and its emission data can be found in Jung et al. (2021) and Vukovich et al. (2018). Since the HCMAQ simulation was based on the year 2016, we applied seasonal scaling for gaseous species based on the comparison", "Using the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model version 4.0, we estimated meteorological fields over the U.S.  $12\\mathrm{km}$  modeling domain (shown in Fig. 1) with the initial and boundary conditions from the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR), which has a  $32\\mathrm{km}$  horizontal resolution and  $3\\mathrm{h}$  of temporal resolution. Detailed configurations of both the CMAQ and WRF models can be found in Table 1. In an effort to enhance model performance in simulating meteorological fields, we applied indirect soil moisture and temperature nudging technique (Pleim and Gilliam, 2009; Pleim and Xiu, 2003) together with a four-", "The variability of satellite-observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns reflects changes in bottom-up emissions and the contribution of background sources in the upper troposphere (Silvern et al., 2019). In Fig. 3, we plotted seasonal averaged changes in observed and simulated tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities in four regions: domainwide (i.e., CONUS), urban and rural regions, and southeastern (SE) areas. We defined an urban area based on the U.S. census-based surrogate, where population density is at least 1000 people per square mile, denoted in red in Fig. 1; and we defined a rural region, designated by gray in Fig. 1, as more than  $20\\mathrm{km}$  away from both urban regions and power plants with a capacity of 1 MW or more, based on the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The southeastern (SE) area (a blue rectangle shown in Fig. 1) denotes an area with dominant lightning-induced (to the total  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions) and biogenic emissions (to the total NMVOCs emissions) over the CONUS. In SE, NMVOCs emissions from biogenic sources are dominant, so we excluded the SE area when we analyzed modeled and observed HCHO columns over urban and rural regions in Fig. 3.", "Fig. 3. The long-term trend in summertime averaged tropospheric (a-1 to h-1)  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and (a-2 to h-2) HCHO columns observed by the OMI satellite (2011-2018) and simulated by the CMAQ model (2011, 2014, and 2017), and their relative changes in the CONUS and urban, rural, and southeastern U.S. regions (shown in Fig. 1). CMAQ simulations were conducted with the prior emission and three background setups (with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and lightning emissions (LNO), with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions, and with profile lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

Estimated coefficients (date) prior to and following the start time of heating.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\\figure_4.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "PM2.5", "SO2", "NO2", "Eastern region", "Western region", "WCHP project"], "concise_summary": "The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.", "references": ["As Fig. 4 shows, the 15 day estimator prior to and following the start of heating varied around a value of zero (0) devoid of a clear trend, and most coefficients were not statistically significant. This result indicated a similarity between pilot cities and non-pilot cities regarding AQI. Despite an abnormal increase 4-6 days following the start time of heating, such differences decreased to a negative value (as seen 2 days prior to the start time of heating), and this trend maintained for the ensuing two weeks. These results were consistent with the hypothesis that the WCHP project generated differential trends in pollutant concentrations between pilot and non-pilot cities. It is noteworthy that the period prior to the start time of heating was implemented and continued for 3-4 days in some cities prior to the formal start time of heating, which may have resulted in the early decrease. Additionally, we also estimated the Heatperiod coefficient 30 days following the start time of heating, which also indicated that effects of the WCHP project existed following the start time of heating.", "where  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  is the set of dummy variables, which represents whether the  $i$ -th city is in a heating period at  $n-1$ -day.  $L_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days prior to the start time of heating, and  $E_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days following the start time of heating. With the omitted time category being the start time of heating,  $L_n$  captures the differences in pollutant indicators between pilot and non-pilot cities relative to differences just prior to the launch of the policy. Here we selected AQI as the air pollutant proxy. Under such specifications, if the signs of the estimated  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  coefficients differ, it can be assumed that changes in pollutant concentrations are not significant.", "As a second robustness check, we constructed a counterfactual test to check whether our results could be driven by other unobserved factors. In doing so, we selected a different date during the non-heating period (May, June, July, August, September, and October) in lieu of the real start date of heating, specifying the interaction term between the treatment variable and the pseudoheating start time variable as Pseudo-HCP. The WCHP project can only take effect during the heating period, while the pseudo-HCP coefficient can reflect the impact of the WCHP project during the non-heating period. If estimated coefficients of pseudo-WCHP are significant and have the same sign as of the true WCHP shown in Table 4, we can conclude that air quality improvements in pilot cities could not be mainly attributed to the WCHP project, and the robustness of the DID model would be inadequate. In this case, improvements may be related to other pollution control policies, such as production restrictions and limits to the number of private vehicle allowed on roads.", "According to the regressions shown in Table 5, only when the start time of heating was specified in October, coefficients of pseudo-WCHP were significantly negative at a  $1\\%$  level, while other estimation results were not significant or had a positive sign. Compared to Table 4, these conflicting results failed to reject the validity of the \"WHCD\" policy. That is to say, the effect of the WCHP project was evident. In fact, we specified the heating start time on the 15th of November in consideration of the \"official\" start time of central heating while ignoring differences caused by scattered coal combustion. In October, some rural areas in the pilot and non-pilot cities began to burn scattered coal for heating, which could cause an evident increase in pollution emissions [47,48]."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

args.query_intent: air pollution spatiotemporal heatmap GIS visualization

orchestrator_plan.reason: 已得到的结果包含单时点或垂直剖面图，未覆盖时空热图。检索GIS热图方法能补充时空分布的直观展示，对可视化需求具有高相关性和新颖性。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
这个检索策略的核心目标是弥补前一次搜索只得到单时点或垂直剖面图的不足，寻找能够直接呈现空气污染时空分布的 GIS 热图（heatmap）方法。为此它以 “air pollution spatiotemporal heatmap GIS visualization” 为查询意图，收获的结果虽大多是高分辨率的空间格网或专题地图，却已经提供了构建时空热图所需的关键底层数据与案例。

检索得到的内容主要围绕以下几类信息展开：  
- **全球与区域排放格网**：如 HTAP v2.2（结果 6、10）详细描述了 0.1°/0.25° 分辨率的全球排放网格，列出美国 EPA、加拿大、欧洲 EMEP‑TNO、亚洲 MIX 等多个权威子库的来源，为制作任意时间截点的污染热图提供了完整的空间基准；  
- **单时点或短期空间分布图**：包括 2010 年亚洲 MIX 排放网格（结果 1）、2008‑2010 区域/全球排放马赛克（结果 2）、美国城市臭氧生产区间的地理图（结果 8）以及 2010 年 8 月 4 日的燃烧率热图（结果 5），这些都展示了如何将栅格数据在 GIS 中可视化，且部分图例已采用热图配色。  
- **基于 GIS 的专题热图实例**：如 2013‑2018 年中国 20 大城市 PM2.5 年均浓度热图（结果 7），显示了随时间演变的热图形式，验证了热图能够直观呈现空气质量的时序趋势；此外还有美国五大都市区臭氧化学 regime 的多年度地图（结果 8），同样提供了时间维度的可视化参考。  
- **其他空间分布案例**：全球火源 NMOG 排放叠加观测站点的地图（结果 4）和京津冀工业热源分布图（结果 9）展示了不同污染源的空间分布方式，提示可将这些要素叠加进综合的空气质量热图。

总体来看，策略成功检索到一批高分辨率的排放格网（尤其是 HTAP v2.2）以及若干已有的 GIS 热图案例，提供了丰富的空间底图、时间截面和可视化配色思路，能够支撑用户进一步制作覆盖更长时间序列的空气污染时空热图。唯一的不足是缺乏连续的多时段热图数据，需要用户自行结合这些格网与时间序列观测（如 PM2.5 年均值）来构建完整的时空可视化。

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

{"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_165", "figure_title": "Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. content.text: The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville). Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont", "figure_id": "img_42", "figure_title": "Fig. 1. Map of the study domain and definition of urban (red) and rural (gray) regions over the CONUS. The yellow triangle symbols represent the location of ozonesonde stations (BLD: Boulder, HVA: Huntsville). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont\\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup", "Describe Data Distribution"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["CONUS", "ozonesonde stations", "CMAQ", "WRF", "urban regions", "rural regions", "Boulder", "Huntsville"], "concise_summary": "The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville).", "inferred_insight": "Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.", "references": ["To compute both atmospheric concentrations of gaseous and particulate pollutants and first-order sensitivity coefficients, required for conducting inverse modeling, we use the Community Multiscale Air Quality model configured with the decoupled direct method in three dimensions (CMAQ DDM-3D) based on CMAQ model version 5.2 (Cohan et al., 2005; Napelenok et al., 2006), developed and released by the EPA. Our study domain covering the CONUS utilizes  $12\\mathrm{km}$  grid spacing with  $459\\times 299$  grid points (shown in Fig. 1), while the vertical extent between the surface and  $100\\mathrm{hPa}$  is discretized with 27 layers of variable thickness. We use carbon bond (CB6r3) for the gas phase and AERO6 for the aerosol chemical mechanism. We obtained the chemical initial and boundary conditions from the CMAQ model with the in-line dust model covering the entire Northern Hemisphere (HCMAQ) (Mathur et al., 2017), providing dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions in a consistent physical configuration. Details of the HCMAQ simulation and its emission data can be found in Jung et al. (2021) and Vukovich et al. (2018). Since the HCMAQ simulation was based on the year 2016, we applied seasonal scaling for gaseous species based on the comparison", "Using the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model version 4.0, we estimated meteorological fields over the U.S.  $12\\mathrm{km}$  modeling domain (shown in Fig. 1) with the initial and boundary conditions from the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR), which has a  $32\\mathrm{km}$  horizontal resolution and  $3\\mathrm{h}$  of temporal resolution. Detailed configurations of both the CMAQ and WRF models can be found in Table 1. In an effort to enhance model performance in simulating meteorological fields, we applied indirect soil moisture and temperature nudging technique (Pleim and Gilliam, 2009; Pleim and Xiu, 2003) together with a four-", "The variability of satellite-observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns reflects changes in bottom-up emissions and the contribution of background sources in the upper troposphere (Silvern et al., 2019). In Fig. 3, we plotted seasonal averaged changes in observed and simulated tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities in four regions: domainwide (i.e., CONUS), urban and rural regions, and southeastern (SE) areas. We defined an urban area based on the U.S. census-based surrogate, where population density is at least 1000 people per square mile, denoted in red in Fig. 1; and we defined a rural region, designated by gray in Fig. 1, as more than  $20\\mathrm{km}$  away from both urban regions and power plants with a capacity of 1 MW or more, based on the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The southeastern (SE) area (a blue rectangle shown in Fig. 1) denotes an area with dominant lightning-induced (to the total  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions) and biogenic emissions (to the total NMVOCs emissions) over the CONUS. In SE, NMVOCs emissions from biogenic sources are dominant, so we excluded the SE area when we analyzed modeled and observed HCHO columns over urban and rural regions in Fig. 3.", "Fig. 3. The long-term trend in summertime averaged tropospheric (a-1 to h-1)  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and (a-2 to h-2) HCHO columns observed by the OMI satellite (2011-2018) and simulated by the CMAQ model (2011, 2014, and 2017), and their relative changes in the CONUS and urban, rural, and southeastern U.S. regions (shown in Fig. 1). CMAQ simulations were conducted with the prior emission and three background setups (with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and lightning emissions (LNO), with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions, and with profile lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

The map underscores the scope of fire influence on NMOG emissions, providing a geographical foundation for analyzing data from air mass sampling campaigns.

evaluation.extracted_insight: Global map overlays measurement sites of ARCTAS, FIREX‑AQ, DC3, and ATTO campaigns on the 1997‑2019 annual mean NMOG fire emissions (GFED4s), illustrating the spatial relationship between fire‑derived NMOG sources and the locations where air‑mass sampling was performed.
evaluation.reason: Offers a clear geospatial framework for linking fire emission inventories to observational datasets, enabling targeted analyses of fire‑influenced NMOG chemistry.

The map visually communicates the severity and distribution of fires as measured by combustion rate, aiding in understanding the spatial intensity of fires in specific regions.

evaluation.extracted_insight: The map visualizes GFASv1.0‑derived carbon combustion rates on 4 August 2010, identifying five 0.5° grid cells where rates exceed 50 g C m⁻² d⁻¹, thus pinpointing the most severe fire hotspots globally.
evaluation.reason: Provides high‑resolution spatial quantification of biomass‑burning emissions with concrete numeric thresholds and source metadata (GFASv1.0), enabling deeper analysis of fire hotspots and linking to emission inventories.

content.text: The HTAP_v2.2 air pollutant grid maps are considered to combine latest available regional information within a complete global data set. The disaggregation by sectors, high spatial and temporal resolution and detailed information on the data sources and references used will provide the user the required transparency. Because HTAP_v2.2 contains primarily official and/or widely used regional emission grid maps, it can be recommended as a global baseline emission inventory, which is regionally accepted as a reference and from which different scenarios assessing emission reduction policies at a global scale could start.

content.text: Heatmap showing annual average PM2.5 concentrations for the top 20 most populated cities in China from 2013 to 2018, highlighting trends in air quality improvements. The figure illustrates significant improvements in PM2.5 concentrations due to strategic ecological campaigns and government interventions in China's major cities.

The strategic promotion of ecological civilization has spurred stronger willingness, clearer targets, more supportive legislation and incentives, and more stringent implementation of control actions from central to local governments. All these factors work together with public engagement to achieve progress in controlling  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution and improving public health and welfare. During the Action Plan 2013-2017,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations deceased by  $23\\%$  on average for all the municipal cities in China [46] (Fig. 5). Reductions for the three key regions were even more significant:  $40\\%$  for BTH,  $34\\%$  for YRD, and  $28\\%$  for PRD. The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration in the PRD region was  $34~\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\cdot \\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , which adhered to NAAQS for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. content.text: Figure 9 displays the ozone production regime changes in five metropolitan areas in the U.S. for the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, using geospatial maps. The maps illustrate shifts in ozone chemical regimes due to variations in NO2 and HCHO columns, highlighting the transition from NOx-saturated to transition or NOx-limited regimes in major cities.
