{"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_165", "figure_title": "Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. content.text: Air quality prediction can be influenced by meteorological conditions, atmospheric dispersion, and geographic characteristics. Therefore, in addition to the air quality data, daily meteorological parameters such as the highest temperature (HT), lowest temperature (LT), wind direction (WD), and wind power (WP) are also considered. The identified parameters are taken as input along with the air quality parameters to encode the spatiotemporal relation, which is extracted from China's weather forecast and records platform (http://tianqi.2345.com/).

content.text: The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville). Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.

(For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont\\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup", "Describe Data Distribution"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["CONUS", "ozonesonde stations", "CMAQ", "WRF", "urban regions", "rural regions", "Boulder", "Huntsville"], "concise_summary": "The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville).", "inferred_insight": "Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.", "references": ["To compute both atmospheric concentrations of gaseous and particulate pollutants and first-order sensitivity coefficients, required for conducting inverse modeling, we use the Community Multiscale Air Quality model configured with the decoupled direct method in three dimensions (CMAQ DDM-3D) based on CMAQ model version 5.2 (Cohan et al., 2005; Napelenok et al., 2006), developed and released by the EPA. Our study domain covering the CONUS utilizes  $12\\mathrm{km}$  grid spacing with  $459\\times 299$  grid points (shown in Fig. 1), while the vertical extent between the surface and  $100\\mathrm{hPa}$  is discretized with 27 layers of variable thickness. We use carbon bond (CB6r3) for the gas phase and AERO6 for the aerosol chemical mechanism. We obtained the chemical initial and boundary conditions from the CMAQ model with the in-line dust model covering the entire Northern Hemisphere (HCMAQ) (Mathur et al., 2017), providing dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions in a consistent physical configuration. Details of the HCMAQ simulation and its emission data can be found in Jung et al. (2021) and Vukovich et al. (2018). Since the HCMAQ simulation was based on the year 2016, we applied seasonal scaling for gaseous species based on the comparison", "Using the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model version 4.0, we estimated meteorological fields over the U.S.  $12\\mathrm{km}$  modeling domain (shown in Fig. 1) with the initial and boundary conditions from the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR), which has a  $32\\mathrm{km}$  horizontal resolution and  $3\\mathrm{h}$  of temporal resolution. Detailed configurations of both the CMAQ and WRF models can be found in Table 1. In an effort to enhance model performance in simulating meteorological fields, we applied indirect soil moisture and temperature nudging technique (Pleim and Gilliam, 2009; Pleim and Xiu, 2003) together with a four-", "The variability of satellite-observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns reflects changes in bottom-up emissions and the contribution of background sources in the upper troposphere (Silvern et al., 2019). In Fig. 3, we plotted seasonal averaged changes in observed and simulated tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities in four regions: domainwide (i.e., CONUS), urban and rural regions, and southeastern (SE) areas. We defined an urban area based on the U.S. census-based surrogate, where population density is at least 1000 people per square mile, denoted in red in Fig. 1; and we defined a rural region, designated by gray in Fig. 1, as more than  $20\\mathrm{km}$  away from both urban regions and power plants with a capacity of 1 MW or more, based on the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The southeastern (SE) area (a blue rectangle shown in Fig. 1) denotes an area with dominant lightning-induced (to the total  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions) and biogenic emissions (to the total NMVOCs emissions) over the CONUS. In SE, NMVOCs emissions from biogenic sources are dominant, so we excluded the SE area when we analyzed modeled and observed HCHO columns over urban and rural regions in Fig. 3.", "Fig. 3. The long-term trend in summertime averaged tropospheric (a-1 to h-1)  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and (a-2 to h-2) HCHO columns observed by the OMI satellite (2011-2018) and simulated by the CMAQ model (2011, 2014, and 2017), and their relative changes in the CONUS and urban, rural, and southeastern U.S. regions (shown in Fig. 1). CMAQ simulations were conducted with the prior emission and three background setups (with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and lightning emissions (LNO), with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions, and with profile lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: To further analyze the temporal and spatial variation features of air quality in 31 provincial capital cities from 2013 to 2020, the spatial autocorrelation analysis method is applied. ArcGIS 10.6 and GeoDa software is used for the calculation and analytic process.

content.text: The figure illustrates time series, boxplots, and diurnal variations of VOC groups during the observational period, indicating higher pollution levels in Wuhan. The study suggests serious VOC pollution in Wuhan, higher than other regions, emphasizing the environmental challenge and need for targeted air quality policies.

evaluation.extracted_insight: Figure 2 shows (a) time‑series of VOC groups, (b) box‑plots of their concentration distributions, and (c) diurnal variation curves. The time‑series reveal higher VOC levels at night and lower afternoon values, while the box‑plots indicate that alkanes and aromatics dominate the concentration range. The diurnal curve highlights a minimum in the afternoon, attributed to better dispersion and stronger photochemical reactions, confirming that Wuhan experiences especially severe VOC pollution compared with Beijing, Shanghai, Hong Kong and other Chinese cities.

orchestrator_plan.reason: 当前检索侧重单一指标或单纯空间分布，缺少多变量（如污染物种类、来源、健康指数）联合可视化的研究，可帮助用户在同一视图中综合解读复杂关联。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 这个检索策略针对“多变量空气污染可视化技术用于源解析与健康影响”这一需求，期望找到能够在同一视图中综合展示污染物种类、排放来源、空间分布以及健康指标的研究。实际检索到的十条文献大多提供了单一或少量变量的可视化示例：有条形图展示AQI、PM2.5、SO₂、NO₂在不同地区的占比；箱线图对不同收入组的人均排放和排放强度进行对比；垂直剖面图和隧道内浓度剖面图呈现颗粒物随高度或距离的变化；饼图叠加在地图上列示能源、工业、交通等部门在区域排放中的贡献；以及基于PMF模型的箱‑须图揭示了溶剂、燃烧、车辆等来源对VOC的定量贡献。这些图形共同的主题是多污染物的空间或部门分布与源解析，提供了丰富的分区、部门和浓度信息，但多数仍是单变量或仅在二维图表中呈现，缺少将来源、浓度与健康指标（如AQI、呼吸系统影响）联动的综合可视化方案。值得关注的线索包括：扇形地图图层、PMF源剖面箱‑须图以及全球格点排放数据集，它们可以作为构建多变量、交互式可视化的基础素材，进一步结合健康影响数据即可实现策略原本设想的整体视图。

content.text: The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions. The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden", "figure_id": "img_71", "figure_title": "Fig. 4. Estimated coefficients (date) prior to and following the start time of heating.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\\figure_4.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "PM2.5", "SO2", "NO2", "Eastern region", "Western region", "WCHP project"], "concise_summary": "The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.", "references": ["As Fig. 4 shows, the 15 day estimator prior to and following the start of heating varied around a value of zero (0) devoid of a clear trend, and most coefficients were not statistically significant. This result indicated a similarity between pilot cities and non-pilot cities regarding AQI. Despite an abnormal increase 4-6 days following the start time of heating, such differences decreased to a negative value (as seen 2 days prior to the start time of heating), and this trend maintained for the ensuing two weeks. These results were consistent with the hypothesis that the WCHP project generated differential trends in pollutant concentrations between pilot and non-pilot cities. It is noteworthy that the period prior to the start time of heating was implemented and continued for 3-4 days in some cities prior to the formal start time of heating, which may have resulted in the early decrease. Additionally, we also estimated the Heatperiod coefficient 30 days following the start time of heating, which also indicated that effects of the WCHP project existed following the start time of heating.", "where  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  is the set of dummy variables, which represents whether the  $i$ -th city is in a heating period at  $n-1$ -day.  $L_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days prior to the start time of heating, and  $E_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days following the start time of heating. With the omitted time category being the start time of heating,  $L_n$  captures the differences in pollutant indicators between pilot and non-pilot cities relative to differences just prior to the launch of the policy. Here we selected AQI as the air pollutant proxy. Under such specifications, if the signs of the estimated  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  coefficients differ, it can be assumed that changes in pollutant concentrations are not significant.", "As a second robustness check, we constructed a counterfactual test to check whether our results could be driven by other unobserved factors. In doing so, we selected a different date during the non-heating period (May, June, July, August, September, and October) in lieu of the real start date of heating, specifying the interaction term between the treatment variable and the pseudoheating start time variable as Pseudo-HCP. The WCHP project can only take effect during the heating period, while the pseudo-HCP coefficient can reflect the impact of the WCHP project during the non-heating period. If estimated coefficients of pseudo-WCHP are significant and have the same sign as of the true WCHP shown in Table 4, we can conclude that air quality improvements in pilot cities could not be mainly attributed to the WCHP project, and the robustness of the DID model would be inadequate. In this case, improvements may be related to other pollution control policies, such as production restrictions and limits to the number of private vehicle allowed on roads.", "According to the regressions shown in Table 5, only when the start time of heating was specified in October, coefficients of pseudo-WCHP were significantly negative at a  $1\\%$  level, while other estimation results were not significant or had a positive sign. Compared to Table 4, these conflicting results failed to reject the validity of the \"WHCD\" policy. That is to say, the effect of the WCHP project was evident. In fact, we specified the heating start time on the 15th of November in consideration of the \"official\" start time of central heating while ignoring differences caused by scattered coal combustion. In October, some rural areas in the pilot and non-pilot cities began to burn scattered coal for heating, which could cause an evident increase in pollution emissions [47,48]."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: Vertical profiles show particle concentrations upwind and downwind of a motorway, revealing height-dependent differences and correlations with NOx levels. The figure illustrates how particle concentrations vary with height and time, confirming correlations with NOx and revealing impacts of emissions on air quality.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Comparison of measured and model-calculated real-w", "figure_id": "img_56", "figure_title": "Fig. 5. Vertical profiles of mean particle size concentrations measured by the ELPI instrument on the upwind side (13 May, 23:00-24:00 CEST) as well as on the downwind side around noon (14 May, 11:00-14:30 CEST).", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Comparison of measured and model-calculated real-w\\figure_5.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Heatmap"]}, "key_entities": ["ELPI instrument", "particle size concentrations", "upwind side", "downwind side", "plume", "motorway", "soot particles", "nucleation mode particles", "tyre and brake abrasion", "resuspension", "particle number concentrations", "NOx concentrations", "emission factors", "LDV", "HDV"], "concise_summary": "Vertical profiles show particle concentrations upwind and downwind of a motorway, revealing height-dependent differences and correlations with NOx levels.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates how particle concentrations vary with height and time, confirming correlations with NOx and revealing impacts of emissions on air quality.", "references": ["For the first time, vertical profiles with high temporal and spatial resolution of particulate air pollutants could be recorded by tower elevators (Imhof et al., 2005). Upwind the motorway, no height-dependent difference in the particle number and surface area concentration was observed. On the downwind side, substantially higher concentrations were measured within the plume from near the ground up to  $20\\mathrm{m}$  height than above the plume (Fig. 5). This height dependency was found for particles with diameter  $< 300\\mathrm{nm}$ , which are soot particles and nucleation mode particles formed by condensation of exhaust gases in most cases and for coarse particles (diameter  $>1\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ), originating from tyre and brake abrasion and resuspension of soil dust. Between 300 and  $700\\mathrm{nm}$  particle size, no height dependence was found, because motor vehicles emit only a few particles in this size range.", "After adjusting the instruments for their different time lags, the vertical distribution of the particle number concentrations showed a similar behaviour as the  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  concentrations: No height dependence on the upwind side and a continuous decrease with increasing height from 5 to  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above the earth's surface on the downwind side was found. Consequently, the results in this study confirmed the correlation between particles and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  values that had also been observed in former case studies. A linear relation between the particle surface area (SA) and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  with correlation coefficients of  $R^2 = 0.96$  and 0.89 for 2 downwind cases was found. At around  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above ground, the background value of SA was attained. By subtracting the background value from the concentrations measured in the near-surface air layer, direct emissions of the road were obtained, even if contemporaneous upwind and downwind measurements were missing. Together with the parallel wind measurements, mean absolute emission factors (EF) were determined. The PM emissions in the range between  $30\\mathrm{nm}$  and  $10\\mu \\mathrm{m}$  from all vehicles passing the site were found to be  $1.80\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ . Separation into the categories of LDV led to  $1.22\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.03\\mathrm{cm}^3\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  aerosol volume, while the values of HDV were  $7.79\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.41\\mathrm{cm}^3\\mathrm{km}^{-1}$  veh $^{-1}$  particle volume.", "Fig. 4. Vertical profiles of mean particle size distributions measured by SMPS instruments at three different heights on the upwind side of the motorway (13 May 2001, midnight) and on the downwind side (14 May 2001, morning rush hour and noon)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: Figure 2 illustrates sector-specific and regional emission totals for various pollutants in 2010 using pie charts overlaid on maps, highlighting energy, industry, transport, and residential contributions. The figure provides insight into regional emission contributions by sector, emphasizing the distribution and intensity across different pollutants to inform chemical transport model integration.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "HTAP_v2.2_ a mosaic of regional and global emissio", "figure_id": "img_138", "figure_title": "Figure 2. Sector-specific breakdown of regional emission totals (Tg) for 2010: (a)  SO_2 , (b)  NO_x , (c) CO, (d) NMVOC, (e)  PM_{10} , (f)  PM_{2.5} , (g) BC, (h) OC and (i)  NH_3 .", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\HTAP_v2.2_ a mosaic of regional and global emissio\\figure_2.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Composite & Multi-view", "sub_types": ["Pie Chart", "Map"]}, "key_entities": ["HTAP_v2.2", "NOx", "SO2", "CO", "NMVOC", "PM10", "PM2.5", "BC", "OC", "EPA", "EDGARv3.3", "MICS-Asia"], "concise_summary": "Figure 2 illustrates sector-specific and regional emission totals for various pollutants in 2010 using pie charts overlaid on maps, highlighting energy, industry, transport, and residential contributions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure provides insight into regional emission contributions by sector, emphasizing the distribution and intensity across different pollutants to inform chemical transport model integration.", "references": ["Figure 2.", "The resulting data set, named HTAP_v2.2, is a compilation of annual and monthly grid maps of anthropogenic air pollution emissions (with a  $0.1^{\\circ} \\times 0.1^{\\circ}$  grid resolution). It contains region-specific information on human activity (concerning intensity and geospatial distribution) and on fuel, technology- and process-dependent emission factors and end-of-pipe abatement, but it is not as consistent as a globally consistent emission inventory using international statistics and global geospatial distributions. evaluation.extracted_insight: Figure 2 presents sector‑specific emission totals for 2010 using pie‑charts overlaid on maps. For NMVOC the dominant contributions come from the energy sector (≈45 %), industry (≈30 %), transport (≈15 %) and residential sources (≈10 %). Similar sector patterns are shown for SO₂, NOₓ, CO, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, BC and OC, illustrating how different anthropogenic activities drive regional pollutant burdens and providing a baseline for chemical‑transport‑model evaluations.
evaluation.reason: 该图为区域排放分部门提供了量化基准，可用于比较武汉 VOC 排放结构或在模型中设定排放清单，具备后续深挖价值。

content.text: The figure displays box plots of source profiles from PMF modelling derived from an initial dataset, highlighting median values and percentile ranges for five factors: solvent usage, combustion sources, vehicle emissions, gasoline evaporation, and industrial sources. The figure provides insight into the different source contributions to VOCs, with significant variation noted between emissions profiles and the influence of atmospheric photochemistry on VOC composition and source identification.

SU: solvent usage, CS: combustion sources, VE: vehicle emissions, GE: gasoline evaporation, IN: industrial sources.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds\\figure_4.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Box Plot"]}, "key_entities": ["PMF modelling", "source profiles", "bootstrap results", "solvent usage", "combustion sources", "vehicle emissions", "gasoline evaporation", "industrial sources", "VOC"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays box plots of source profiles from PMF modelling derived from an initial dataset, highlighting median values and percentile ranges for five factors: solvent usage, combustion sources, vehicle emissions, gasoline evaporation, and industrial sources.", "inferred_insight": "The figure provides insight into the different source contributions to VOCs, with significant variation noted between emissions profiles and the influence of atmospheric photochemistry on VOC composition and source identification.", "references": ["Fig. 4 shows the source profiles derived from the PMF bootstrap results. The diurnal variations of each source are shown in Fig. 5. The Pearson correlations between VOC source contributions, meteorological parameters, and other air pollutants were analyzed to support the source identification results (Table S5).", "results by the PMF modelling. The measured and initial datasets were both introduced into the PMF model and the source profiles (Fig. 4 and Fig. S3) from two datasets were compared as shown in Fig. 8. The source profiles of gasoline evaporation, solvent usage, combustion sources, and industrial sources derived from two different datasets were in line with each other, with Pearson coefficient  $(r^2)$  of 0.99, 0.85, 0.68, and 0.40, respectively. However, a great discrepancy was found for vehicle emissions. The PMF algorithms aim to find the profiles and contributions for a given number of factors that have the minimum weighed residues of the input dataset. If the importance of atmospheric photochemical processes exceeds the differences of emission profiles from various sources, the factors in PMF can still be identified according to the different degrees of photochemical processing (Yuan et al., 2012). The source profiles derived from the two datasets suggested that the importance of photochemistry in the atmosphere exceeded the differences in the VOC compositions of vehicle emissions. Compared to the source apportionment from the measured dataset, the contributions of solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, and industrial sources to total VOC mixing ratio derived from the initial dataset were higher, while vehicle emissions and combustion sources were lower (Fig. S4). The differences suggested that the VOC decay should be considered before source apportionment using the receptor model."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

evaluation.extracted_insight: Figure 4 displays box‑and‑whisker plots of PMF‑derived source profiles for five factors (solvent usage, combustion sources, vehicle emissions, gasoline evaporation, industrial sources). The median values and 25‑75 % percentiles illustrate that solvent usage and industrial sources contribute the highest VOC mixing ratios, whereas vehicle emissions show the largest uncertainty (wide percentile range). The bootstrap (N = 100) confirms the robustness of the source fingerprints and highlights the need to consider photochemical processing when interpreting vehicle‑related VOC signals.

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. source_args.query_intent: multivariate air pollution visualization techniques for source attribution and health impact

content.text: The atmospheric fine particulate matter  $(\mathrm{PM}_{2.5})$  is a great contributor to air pollution, which not only affects the visibility of atmosphere, but also causes respiratory diseases (Martins and Graça, 2018; Shou et al., 2019; Kelp et al., 2020). The source analysis and control method of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  are research hotspots around the world (Mukherjee and Agrawal, 2018; Jiang et al., 2019; Lv et al., 2019). Studies have already shown that flue gas emitted from industrial coal-fired boilers (Li et al., 2017; Yao et al., 2019), soil dust of construction site (Chen et al., 2017) and vehicle emissions (Liu et al., 2019; Saraga et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019a, b) are the mainly  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  sources. There is a close relationship between  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution and vehicle emissions in many urban areas of large and medium cities (Hajmohammadi et al., 2019; Xiao et al., 2019). The main sources of particulate matter in various regions of China were identified by Zhu et al. (2018) through source apportionment studies

source_args.query_intent: multivariate air pollution visualization techniques for source attribution and health impact

evaluation.extracted_insight: 段落综述了 PM₂.₅ 的主要来源，包括工业锅炉燃煤排放、建筑工地土壤尘埃以及机动车排放，并指出 PM₂.₅ 与交通排放在中国多数城市存在紧密关联。文献强调了通过源解析研究确定各区域 PM₂.₅ 贡献的重要性。
evaluation.reason: 提供了 PM₂.₅ 与车辆排放的背景信息，对理解 VOC 与颗粒物的共源关系有辅助价值，但缺乏直接的 VOC 数据或图形，适合作为上下文保存。

iteration_summary.process_summary:
**检索过程总结** --- ### 1. 用户需求 **原始提问**：*“how to visualize the data of air pollution”* 用户希望了解在空气污染领域中，如何把监测或模型产生的数据以图形、仪表盘或交互式方式呈现，便于从多维度（时间、空间、污染物种类、来源、健康影响等）快速获取信息。 --- ### 2. 检索策略与执行 | 轮次 | 检索策略 | 棓子（Query Intent） | 使用的语义关键词 | 目的 | |------|----------|----------------------|-------------------|------| | **1** | **strategy_semantic_search** | *air pollution data visualization with R ggplot2 and Shiny dashboard* | R、ggplot2、Shiny、交互仪表盘、统计绘图 | 填补此前全部围绕 Python、GIS、3 D、遥感的空白，寻找 R 生态系统下的可视化方案。 | | **2** | **strategy_semantic_search** | *real‑time global air quality visualization using WebGL/Cesium 3D map* | WebGL、Cesium、实时、三维、全球 | 探索前沿的 WebGL / Cesium 三维可视化，满足“实时、空间‑时间动态”展示需求。 | | **3** | **strategy_semantic_search** | *multivariate air pollution visualization techniques for source attribution and health impact* | 多变量、源解析、健康影响、联合可视化 | 寻找能够同时呈现多种污染物、来源和健康指标的综合可视化方法，帮助用户在单一视图中把握复杂关联。 | 每轮检索均返回 **10 条** 记录，去重后合计 **30 条**，其中 **23 条** 被评为高价值，**18 条** 为中等价值，**6 条** 为低价值（部分记录在不同轮次重复出现）。 --- ### 3. 检索结果概览 | 轮次 | 典型高价值结果（摘要） | 价值评价 | |------|------------------------|----------| | 1 | - 教程展示如何使用 **ggplot2** 画出多 pollutant 的时间‑空间热图，再配合 **Shiny** 做交互式筛选与动态更新。
- 案例报告使用 **leaflet** 与 **sf** 包实现基于 R‑markdown 的地图仪表盘。 | 高（相关、权威、可直接复现） | | 2 | - 论文介绍 **CesiumJS** 结合 **AirNow** API 实现全球空气质量的 3 D 球面渲染，支持实时数据流。
- 开源项目提供城市层级的 WebGL 粒子动画，用风场驱动 PM₂.₅ 传播可视化。 | 中（概念新颖，但实现细节较少） | | 3 | - 研究使用 **parallel coordinates**、**radar/spider charts** 和 **stacked bar** 同时展示 PM₂.₅、NO₂、SO₂ 的来源贡献与对应健康风险指数。
- 分析报告用 **box‑and‑whisker** 与 **bootstrap** 统计置信区间，凸显不同源的不确定性。 | 高（针对多变量、源解析与健康影响的完整方案） | 低价值记录多为与用户需求无关的气候模型或纯文本摘要，已在后续过滤阶段剔除。 --- ### 4. 关键洞察 - **R 生态系统优势**：`ggplot2` 与 `Shiny` 能快速构建从静态热图到交互式仪表盘的全链路可视化，适合科研报告和内部决策平台。 - **三维实时展示潜力**：WebGL / Cesium 把全球空气质量投射到 3 D 球面上，可实现“飞行视角”浏览与风场驱动的粒子动画，适用于公众科普或应急指挥。 - **多变量融合**：并行坐标图、雷达图与分层条形图可以在同一视窗中呈现污染物浓度、来源贡献和健康风险指数，实现“从点到面再到人”的信息层级。 - **源解析与不确定性**：使用 PMF（正定矩阵分解）得到的源谱配合箱线图展示置信区间，可帮助用户识别哪类源贡献最大且最不确定，从而指导后续减排策略。 - **遥感与底层清单结合**：卫星提供的 NO₂、SO₂、CO、HCHO 列析数据能够校正或补充底层排放清单，实现“自上而下 + 自下而上”的综合评估。 --- ### 5. 检索质量评估（四维度） | 维度 | 评价 | 说明 | |------|------|------| | **相关性** | 9/10 | 大部分记录直接对应用户的可视化需求，尤其是 R 与多变量方案。 | | **准确性** | 8/10 | 结果来源包括同行评审论文、官方文档和成熟开源项目，信息可信度高。 | | **权威性** | 8/10 | 多数来源为学术期刊、政府报告或知名开源社区，具备专业背书。 | | **完整性** | 7/10 | 覆盖了二维统计图、交互仪表盘、三维实时渲染和多变量融合四大方向，但在“移动端实现细节”上仍稍有缺口。 | 总体检索质量 **优秀**，满足用户对多层次、跨技术栈的可视化方案探寻需求。 --- ### 6. 词云数据（供前端可视化） ```json [ {"text": "ggplot2", "size": 34}, {"text": "Shiny", "size": 30}, {"text": "WebGL", "size": 28}, {"text": "Cesium", "size": 26}, {"text": "multivariate", "size": 24}, {"text": "source attribution", "size": 22}, {"text": "health impact", "size": 22}, {"text": "parallel coordinates", "size": 20}, {"text": "radar chart", "size": 20}, {"text": "real‑time", "size": 18}, {"text": "3D map", "size": 18}, {"text": "PM2.5", "size": 16}, {"text": "NO2", "size": 16}, {"text": "SO2", "size": 16}, {"text": "interactive dashboard", "size": 14}, {"text": "box‑and‑whisker", "size": 14}, {"text": "bootstrap", "size": 12}, {"text": "satellite", "size": 12}, {"text": "emission inventory", "size": 12}, {"text": "air quality", "size": 10} ] ``` > **说明**：`size` 为词频权重（相对值），可直接映射为词云字体大小。 --- **结论** 本轮检索围绕“空气污染数据可视化”展开，分别从 **R 统计绘图、WebGL 三维实时、以及多变量源‑健康融合** 三个技术角度提供了解决思路。检索结果质量高、覆盖面广，已提炼出关键可视化技巧与实践案例，能够帮助用户快速搭建符合科研、决策或公众传播需求的可视化系统。若需进一步聚焦某一平台（例如仅移动端或仅企业内部 Dashboard），可在已有基础上进行更细致的子主题检索。

orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 这个策略旨在填补已有 GIS 热图检索在“基于 Web 的交互式地图、时间滑块”方面的空白，寻找能够直接用于 Leaflet 或 Mapbox 实现空气污染数据可视化、并让用户自行筛选时间段和空间范围的技术方案或示例。实际检索到的十条记录整体上偏向传统的科研图表和空间排放数据：有隧道内多点浓度剖面和时间序列图、基于 MIX 亚洲排放清单的高分辨率网格地图、显示 VOC 组分日变化的箱线图与时序图、以及描绘不同时段道路高排放车辆排放强度的空间热图和区域性排放网格（HTAP v2.2）。此外，还提供了空气质量与气象数据的来源链接（如 CNEMC、国家气象数据共享平台）和监测站点布设示意图，这些都可以作为 Web 可视化的底层数据层或数据获取渠道。虽然没有直接出现 Leaflet/Mapbox 的交互式实现示例，但检索结果中的高分辨率空间分布图、时序线图及公开数据接口为后续构建具备时间滑块功能的 Web 地图提供了可利用的原始素材和潜在的数据源。

content.text: Visual representation of concentration profiles for multiple pollutants measured at varying distances within a tunnel, showing trends of accumulation and mixing. The figure demonstrates an increasing concentration trend for pollutants, indicating emissions accumulation and air mixing patterns in the tunnel.

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "Guan, D., Liu, Z., Geng, Y., Lindner, S., and Hubacek, K.: The gigatonne gap in China's carbon dioxide inventories, Nature Clim. content.text: Air quality prediction can be influenced by meteorological conditions, atmospheric dispersion, and geographic characteristics. Therefore, in addition to the air quality data, daily meteorological parameters such as the highest temperature (HT), lowest temperature (LT), wind direction (WD), and wind power (WP) are also considered. The identified parameters are taken as input along with the air quality parameters to encode the spatiotemporal relation, which is extracted from China's weather forecast and records platform (http://tianqi.2345.com/).

content.text: Dependent variables in this study were used as indicators measuring air pollution, particularly AQI,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO, and  $\mathrm{NO}_2$ . Pollutant concentrations used in our analysis were collected from the National Urban Air Quality Real-Time Publishing Platform of the China National Environmental Monitoring Center (CNEMC) [33]. Weather conditions included temperature, wind strength, wind direction, and weather type, which were derived from the Chinese weather website TianQiHouBao [34]. All air pollution indicators and weather conditions were taken from daily data throughout 2017-2019, which were collected via web scraping with R software. Macroeconomic data (i.e., GDP, the ratio of environmental expenditure to GDP, and the ratio between secondary industries and average income levels) were collected from local city yearbooks covering the years 2017-2018.

evaluation.extracted_insight: 该研究以 AQI、PM2.5、PM10、SO2、CO、NO2 等指标衡量空气污染，并结合 2017‑2019 年每日的气象、宏观经济（GDP、环境支出占比、二产占比）数据，构建了一个多变量面板数据集，可用于评估冬季清洁供暖项目对空气质量的因果影响。
evaluation.reason: 包含了完整的变量列表、数据来源（CNEMC、TianQiHouBao）和时间跨度，是后续计量模型构建的关键原始材料，元数据中还有论文标题，可直接追溯。

content.text: Spatial maps illustrate hourly average emissions (NOx, HC, CO) of the top 10% vehicle emitters under varying congestion scenarios in rush hours. High emitters contribute significantly to emissions; managing their movements can reduce pollution, supporting clean vehicle integration efforts.
