content.text: The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions. The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden", "figure_id": "img_71", "figure_title": "Fig. 4. Estimated coefficients (date) prior to and following the start time of heating.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\\figure_4.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "PM2.5", "SO2", "NO2", "Eastern region", "Western region", "WCHP project"], "concise_summary": "The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.", "references": ["As Fig. 4 shows, the 15 day estimator prior to and following the start of heating varied around a value of zero (0) devoid of a clear trend, and most coefficients were not statistically significant. This result indicated a similarity between pilot cities and non-pilot cities regarding AQI. Despite an abnormal increase 4-6 days following the start time of heating, such differences decreased to a negative value (as seen 2 days prior to the start time of heating), and this trend maintained for the ensuing two weeks. These results were consistent with the hypothesis that the WCHP project generated differential trends in pollutant concentrations between pilot and non-pilot cities. It is noteworthy that the period prior to the start time of heating was implemented and continued for 3-4 days in some cities prior to the formal start time of heating, which may have resulted in the early decrease. Additionally, we also estimated the Heatperiod coefficient 30 days following the start time of heating, which also indicated that effects of the WCHP project existed following the start time of heating.", "where  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  is the set of dummy variables, which represents whether the  $i$ -th city is in a heating period at  $n-1$ -day.  $L_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days prior to the start time of heating, and  $E_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days following the start time of heating. With the omitted time category being the start time of heating,  $L_n$  captures the differences in pollutant indicators between pilot and non-pilot cities relative to differences just prior to the launch of the policy. Here we selected AQI as the air pollutant proxy. Under such specifications, if the signs of the estimated  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  coefficients differ, it can be assumed that changes in pollutant concentrations are not significant.", "As a second robustness check, we constructed a counterfactual test to check whether our results could be driven by other unobserved factors. In doing so, we selected a different date during the non-heating period (May, June, July, August, September, and October) in lieu of the real start date of heating, specifying the interaction term between the treatment variable and the pseudoheating start time variable as Pseudo-HCP. The WCHP project can only take effect during the heating period, while the pseudo-HCP coefficient can reflect the impact of the WCHP project during the non-heating period. If estimated coefficients of pseudo-WCHP are significant and have the same sign as of the true WCHP shown in Table 4, we can conclude that air quality improvements in pilot cities could not be mainly attributed to the WCHP project, and the robustness of the DID model would be inadequate. In this case, improvements may be related to other pollution control policies, such as production restrictions and limits to the number of private vehicle allowed on roads.", "According to the regressions shown in Table 5, only when the start time of heating was specified in October, coefficients of pseudo-WCHP were significantly negative at a  $1\\%$  level, while other estimation results were not significant or had a positive sign. Compared to Table 4, these conflicting results failed to reject the validity of the \"WHCD\" policy. That is to say, the effect of the WCHP project was evident. In fact, we specified the heating start time on the 15th of November in consideration of the \"official\" start time of central heating while ignoring differences caused by scattered coal combustion. In October, some rural areas in the pilot and non-pilot cities began to burn scattered coal for heating, which could cause an evident increase in pollution emissions [47,48]."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: The figure displays the location and setup of air pollution monitoring sites within East Yan'an Road Tunnel, Shanghai. It includes schematic representations of the tunnel and monitoring locations. The schematic provides spatial context for the air quality monitoring study, highlighting the strategic placement of sensors to capture key pollutants from vehicle emissions in a high-traffic urban tunnel.

{"paper_title": "Evaluation of emission factors for light-duty gaso", "figure_id": "img_95", "figure_title": "Fig. 2. Schematic diagram of the monitoring site in the East Yan'an Road Tunnel, Shanghai.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Evaluation of emission factors for light-duty gaso\\figure_2.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Diagrams & Schematics", "sub_types": ["Conceptual Diagram"]}, "key_entities": ["East Yan'an Road Tunnel", "Shanghai", "Huangpu River", "air pollution monitoring", "vehicle exhaust", "sensors", "NO", "NO2", "CO", "PM2.5"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays the location and setup of air pollution monitoring sites within East Yan'an Road Tunnel, Shanghai. It includes schematic representations of the tunnel and monitoring locations.", "inferred_insight": "The schematic provides spatial context for the air quality monitoring study, highlighting the strategic placement of sensors to capture key pollutants from vehicle emissions in a high-traffic urban tunnel.", "references": ["A tunnel in the city center of Shanghai $(31.24^{\\circ}\\mathrm{N}, 121.50^{\\circ}\\mathrm{E})$ was selected to conduct air pollution monitoring. The tunnel is called East Yan'an Road tunnel, is an underwater tunnel crossing the Huangpu River in Shanghai. It has two bores with two lanes for each direction. The total length is $2,107\\mathrm{m}$ ; cross-sectional area is $33.75\\mathrm{m}^2$ . To continuously monitor major air pollutants from vehicle exhaust, we installed two integrated air pollution monitoring units at $150\\mathrm{m}$ away from the inlet and outlet of the tunnel, respectively. The height of the unit was about $1.2\\mathrm{m}$ . Fig. 2 shows the location of the tunnel and the monitoring sites. There were 4 sensors which could separately monitor NO, $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ , CO, and $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ concentrations in each unit. NO, $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ , and CO were detected using electrochemical method. Their detection limits were $0.01\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , $0.01\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , and $0.15\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , respectively. $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ was detected by light scattering method. Its resolution was $0.1\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . The monitoring data was continuously transmitted to a remote database through wireless network. The system was built in July 2016 and has been operated by now. To ensure the reliability of monitoring", "Vehicle flow and average speed in the tunnel were detected by a buried induction coil and transmitted to a data platform in real time. The fleet compositions were recognized by automatic acquisition of vehicle license plate information through a high-definition video in the tunnel. Fig. 4 shows the average traffic flow and fleet composition in each hour of weekday and weekend. The data between August and November of 2016 were used in this study. Daily average vehicle flow and speed were $34,818 \\pm 2,214 \\mathrm{veh} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ and $33.47 \\pm 7.95 \\mathrm{km} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ on weekday and were $30,818 \\pm 1,848 \\mathrm{veh} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ and $33.86 \\pm 8.83 \\mathrm{km} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ on weekend. Vehicle flows in peak hour were $2,173 \\pm 161$ and $1,952 \\pm 68 \\mathrm{veh} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ on weekday and weekend, respectively. East Yan'an Road tunnel was located in the city center of Shanghai, where heavy-duty diesel vehicles (HDDVs) were restricted to access except for some medium-duty diesel vehicles (MDDVs). Therefore, fleet in the tunnel was mainly comprised of light-duty gasoline vehicles, which accounted for $93.7 \\pm 2.2\\%$ and $94.6 \\pm 1.9\\%$ of the total on weekday and weekend, respectively. The", "tunnel in Taiwan in 2006. The CO, $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ EFs were $0.91 \\pm 0.47 - 1.47 \\pm 0.63$ , $0.15 \\pm 0.07 - 0.33 \\pm 0.17\\mathrm{gkm}^{-1} \\cdot \\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ , and $2 \\pm 2 - 4 \\pm 3\\mathrm{mgkm}^{-1} \\cdot \\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ , respectively. Their results were the lowest compared with other tunnel studies in China mainly because Taiwan implemented Tier 1 and Tier 2 emission standards in 1999 and 2008, earlier than the mainland of China. Liu et al. (2015a,b) and Deng et al. (2015) measured CO and $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ EFs in East Yan'an Road tunnel in 2012. CO EF decreased by about $30\\%$ compared with our result in 2016. $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ EF also declined if we considered $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ was similar to $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ for vehicle exhaust. The enforcement of vehicle emission control strategies should be the main reason for the decrease. Between 2012 and 2016, Shanghai implemented the emission standards of LDGVs from Euro 4 to Euro 5, eliminated Euro 0 gasoline vehicles from the whole city, and restricted Euro 1 gasoline vehicles in urban area. CO and $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ EFs in Yingpan Road tunnel were $0.75 \\pm 0.56 - 6.05 \\pm 5.94$ and $0.12 \\pm 0.02 - 0.82 \\pm 0.76\\mathrm{gkm}^{-1} \\cdot \\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ (Deng et al., 2015). Our results were relatively lower than the average value of Yingpan Road tunnel. The fractions of gasoline and diesel vehicles have influences on EFs in the tunnels. CO, $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ EFs in Zhujiang tunnel were much higher than those in our study since there was higher proportion of diesel vehicles (Liu et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2015b). Correspondingly, KXL tunnel had lower $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ EF due to its high proportion of gasoline vehicles (Cui et al., 2016).", "

content.text: The figure illustrates the methodology framework for fusing multisource data to estimate daily emissions from coal-fired power plants in China, detailing processes like outlier detection and imputation. The framework effectively combines real-time emission data with annual figures to generate accurate daily emissions, compensating for data gaps and variations across plants.

{"paper_title": "Daily Emission Patterns of Coal-Fired Power Plants", "figure_id": "img_61", "figure_title": "Figure 1. Methodology framework for fusing multisource data to derive daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plants in China.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Daily Emission Patterns of Coal-Fired Power Plants\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Methodology & Design", "sub_roles": ["Elucidate Model Architecture", "Illustrate Algorithm Process"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Diagrams & Schematics", "sub_types": ["Flowchart"]}, "key_entities": ["CEMS", "CPED", "emission rates", "flue gas volume", "outlier detection", "imputation"], "concise_summary": "The figure illustrates the methodology framework for fusing multisource data to estimate daily emissions from coal-fired power plants in China, detailing processes like outlier detection and imputation.", "inferred_insight": "The framework effectively combines real-time emission data with annual figures to generate accurate daily emissions, compensating for data gaps and variations across plants.", "references": ["Figure 1 shows the methodology framework in this study, including the input database and processes to fuse multisource data to derive daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plant emissions.", "In this work, we develop a framework that fuses plant-level daily profiles from CEMS with yearly emission estimates from CPED to obtain daily emissions (Figure 1). According to guideline HJ/T 75-2017, the maximum volume fraction of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions from the combustion of specific fuels remains stable, so we use daily profiles of flue gas volume that takes the oxygen content into account to describe daily variations in  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions. Daily profiles of emission rates from CEMS are used for major air pollutants of  $\mathrm{SO}_{2}, \mathrm{NO}_{x},$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ .", "# Daily Emission Patterns of Coal-Fired Power Plants in China Based on Multisource Data Fusion", "ABSTRACT: Daily emission estimates are essential for tracking the dynamic changes in emission sources. In this work, we estimate daily emissions of coal-fired power plants in China during 2017-2020 by combining information from the unit-based China coal-fired Power plant Emissions Database (CPED) and real-time measurements from continuous emission monitoring systems (CEMS). We develop a step-by-step method to screen outliers and impute missing values for data from CEMS. Then, plant-level daily profiles of flue gas volume and emissions obtained from CEMS are coupled with annual emissions from CPED to derive daily emissions. Reasonable agreement is found between emission variations and available statistics (i.e., monthly power generation and daily coal consumption). Daily power emissions are in the range of 6267-12,994, 0.4-1.3, 6.5-12.0, and  $2.5 - 6.8\mathrm{Gg}$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{x}$ , and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , respectively, with high emissions in winter and summer caused", "is used to quantify monthly variations in power emissions in a unit-level power plant emission inventory in China.17 Indicator data such as power load curves are employed to indicate daily or hourly dynamics of power emissions.1,18 Recent studies have also attempted to estimate dynamically updated daily power emissions,19-22 which are largely stimulated by the demand for quantifying the evolution of emissions during fast-evolving events (e.g., COVID-19 pandemic). Liu et al.19,20 used daily coal consumption from six major power generation groups in China to estimate daily updated power emissions. However, the daily coal consumption data only covers 62 power plants in China, and the activity indicator-based method is relatively indirect and lacks independent evaluations against observations. Studies utilizing satellite observations to estimate daily emission changes are faced with the challenges of differentiating power emissions from the total21,23 or deriving daily power emissions on continuous time scales.24,25 For example, Ding et al.23 used the  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission reductions derived from", "In this work, taking advantage of a well-validated unit-based power emission database covering China and the high temporal resolution of CEMS data, we attempt to construct daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plants in China based on multisource data fusion. Careful screening of outliers and imputation of missing values are conducted for the flue gas volume and emission data. Then, the plant-level daily profiles (i.e., coefficients for day-to-day variations in a year) from CEMS are combined with the unit-level annual emissions from", "2.1.1. Unit-Level Emission Database of Coal-Fired Power Plants in China. The CPED, $^{17}$  developed by Tsinghua University, is a high-resolution emission inventory that provides year-by-year emissions from coal-fired power plants since 1990 in China. Established based on detailed and unit-level information from the Ministry of Ecology and Environment (MEE, undisclosed data), CPED contains unit locations, operational status, unit capacity, coal consumption, control technologies, and emission factors, which enable accurate spatial allocation and emission estimates for power plants. More details about CPED can be found in the paper by Liu et al. $^{16}$  Previous studies have verified that the magnitude and trends of power emissions in CPED are in good agreement with top-down estimates from satellite measurements. $^{43,50}$  Liu et al. $^{43}$  compared  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions in CPED with those based on satellite data and found rather good agreement with the relative differences of  $4 \pm 18\%$ . CPED used here covers 30 provinces (except Hong Kong, Macao, Taiwan, and Tibet), 3948 power plants, and 8722 in-use electricity-generating units from 2017 to 2020.", "In this study, data from CPED and CEMS are fused to improve the daily estimation of emissions from coal-fired power units in China. The comprehensive, highly resolved emission database could capture the dynamic changes in emissions during typical socioeconomic events and could be applied to providing more accurate inputs for CTMs and guiding future policies.", "In previous studies, monthly activity data (e.g., electricity generation and fuel consumption) $^{4}$  and invariable parameters considering factors such as working times $^{1,57}$  have been widely used to disaggregate annual power emissions into monthly and daily scales, respectively. Figure 5 shows comparisons of the multiscale temporal profiles from CEMS with the widely used temporal profiles. At the monthly scale (Figure 5a), the comparison between national average profiles obtained from CEMS and electricity generation shows good agreement ( $R$  between 0.74 and 0.97). The results imply that the commonly used activity indicators are reliable at monthly scale when direct emission measurements are unavailable. At the weekly scale, previous studies $^{1,57}$  use fixed parameters that do not vary over time to allocate emissions from weekly totals to daily scales, considering the empirical production schedule and a few observations, which tend to fail to reflect fluctuations caused by unexpected events. Distinct weekly patterns of power emissions in previous studies $^{1,57}$  are presented in Figure 5b, which are manifested as strong intensity of production activities on weekdays and weak intensity on weekends. Conversely, the weekly profiles from CEMS exhibit no obvious weekend effects, showing that the widely used profiles might exaggerate the differences between weekdays and weekends. Indeed, in many developed countries, $^{58}$  power demand on working days is usually higher than that on weekends. However, owing to different lifestyle, economic, and cultural background, there is no significant difference in electricity demand and power generation between weekdays and weekends in China. And unlike peaking power plants (e.g., natural gas power plants and hydroelectric facilities), coal-fired", "In this work, we estimate daily emissions from coal-fired power plants in China during 2017-2020 through data fusion of a well-validated unit-based power emission database and high-temporal-resolution measurements from CEMS. A step-by-step method is developed to screen outliers and impute missing values for data from CEMS, and sensitivity tests show that monthly and weekly temporal profiles by different imputation methods are in good agreement ( $R = 0.90 - 0.98$  for national scale,  $R = 0.61 - 0.92$  for provincial level). We find that the temporal profiles from CEMS agree well with available statistical data like monthly power generation and daily coal consumption, with  $R$  greater than 0.7 for 16-27 out of 30 provinces at the monthly scale and  $R$  up to 0.83 at the daily scale. Daily power emissions from the fused database are in the range of 6267-12,994, 0.4-1.3, 6.5-12.0, and  $2.5 - 6.8\mathrm{Gg}$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , respectively, during 2017-2020, reflecting higher emissions in winter and summer caused by heating and cooling demand and lower ones resulting from holidays. Our estimates are also able to capture the sudden changes in daily power emissions during typical socioeconomic events, including the much slower rebound of power emissions after the Spring Festival holiday due to the impact of COVID-19, higher daily  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions from thermal power generation in Yunnan owing to a severe drought, and significant decline of power emissions in Wuhan caused by a short-term emission control during the 7th CISM Military World Games. We also find that previous studies (e.g.,  $\mathrm{EDGAR}^1$ ) that apply fixed parameters to allocate weekly power emission totals to daily scales exaggerate the differences between weekdays and weekends. This work provides a framework to propagate information from real-time emission measurements to high-temporal-resolution emission estimates, which will support dynamic emission tracking and air pollution modeling.", "(6) Nassar, R.; Napier-Linton, L.; Gurney, K. source_args.query_intent: low‑cost sensor network air quality data visualization using Grafana and InfluxDB dashboards

evaluation.extracted_insight: 图 1 展示了一个多源数据融合框架，用于将 CEMS 实时排放数据与 CPED 年度排放数据库结合，通过异常值检测和缺失值插补，生成中国燃煤电厂的日尺度排放估计，能够弥补数据缺口并提升每日排放的准确性。
evaluation.reason: 该图为核心方法学示意，提供了后续深入分析的路径（如模型架构、算法流程），且元数据包含论文 ID 与图表来源，价值极高。

content.text: The figure shows air pollutant concentrations (NO, NO2, CO, PM2.5) at the inlet and outlet of a Shanghai tunnel over time using line charts. The figure illustrates higher pollutant concentrations at the tunnel outlet, highlighting the impact of vehicle emissions within the tunnel.

Air pollutant concentrations at inlet and outlet of the tunnel. (a) NO; (b)  NO_2 ; (c) CO; (d)  PM_{2.5} .", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Evaluation of emission factors for light-duty gaso\\figure_3.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Present Core Findings"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["NO", "NO2", "CO", "PM2.5", "air pollutant concentrations", "tunnel", "inlet", "outlet"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows air pollutant concentrations (NO, NO2, CO, PM2.5) at the inlet and outlet of a Shanghai tunnel over time using line charts.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates higher pollutant concentrations at the tunnel outlet, highlighting the impact of vehicle emissions within the tunnel.", "references": ["data, we regularly carried out maintenance of the monitoring units, calibrated the sensors, and cleaned the sampling pipes. Fig. 3 shows a fragment of NO,  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ , CO, and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations at inlet and outlet of the tunnel.", "A tunnel in the city center of Shanghai  $(31.24^{\\circ}\\mathrm{N}, 121.50^{\\circ}\\mathrm{E})$  was selected to conduct air pollution monitoring. The tunnel is called East Yan'an Road tunnel, is an underwater tunnel crossing the Huangpu River in Shanghai. It has two bores with two lanes for each direction. The total length is  $2,107\\mathrm{m}$ ; cross-sectional area is  $33.75\\mathrm{m}^2$ . To continuously monitor major air pollutants from vehicle exhaust, we installed two integrated air pollution monitoring units at  $150\\mathrm{m}$  away from the inlet and outlet of the tunnel, respectively. The height of the unit was about  $1.2\\mathrm{m}$ . Fig. 2 shows the location of the tunnel and the monitoring sites. There were 4 sensors which could separately monitor NO,  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ , CO, and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in each unit. NO,  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ , and CO were detected using electrochemical method. Their detection limits were  $0.01\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ,  $0.01\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , and  $0.15\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , respectively.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was detected by light scattering method. Its resolution was  $0.1\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . The monitoring data was continuously transmitted to a remote database through wireless network. The system was built in July 2016 and has been operated by now. To ensure the reliability of monitoring", "Where,  $EF(\\mathrm{g}\\cdot \\mathrm{km}^{-1})$  is the average emission factor per vehicle per kilometer of fleet in the tunnel.  $C_{outlet}$  and  $C_{inlet}$ $(\\mathrm{mg}\\cdot \\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  are air pollutant concentrations at outlet and inlet of the tunnel.  $A(\\mathrm{m}^2)$  is the cross-sectional area of the tunnel  $(\\mathrm{A} = 33.75\\mathrm{m}^2)$ .  $\\nu (\\mathrm{m}\\cdot \\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  is air velocity parallel to the tunnel monitored by an operation management company of the tunnel. Daily average air velocity was"], "chunk_type": "figure"}

source_args.query_intent: low‑cost sensor network air quality data visualization using Grafana and InfluxDB dashboards

content.text: The figure illustrates time series, boxplots, and diurnal variations of VOC groups during the observational period, indicating higher pollution levels in Wuhan. The study suggests serious VOC pollution in Wuhan, higher than other regions, emphasizing the environmental challenge and need for targeted air quality policies.

content.text: one-third of cities. Due to the shutdown of industrial activities and traffic volume, an obvious decline in primary air pollutants was observed in most Chinese cities in 2020 (Liu et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2021). This will undoubtedly cause high uncertainty while investigating the effect of CCTPs on air quality. Therefore, the following data is only extracted from 2013 to 2019, and the AQI in 2020 is predicted through a deep learning network. Subsequently, the investigated data covers 8 years before and after the policy node, namely from 2013 to 2020, which is adequate for policy evaluation (Yu et al., 2021). In the following, air quality and meteorological data are obtained via a web crawler.

content.text: Air quality prediction can be influenced by meteorological conditions, atmospheric dispersion, and geographic characteristics. Therefore, in addition to the air quality data, daily meteorological parameters such as the highest temperature (HT), lowest temperature (LT), wind direction (WD), and wind power (WP) are also considered. The identified parameters are taken as input along with the air quality parameters to encode the spatiotemporal relation, which is extracted from China's weather forecast and records platform (http://tianqi.2345.com/).

args.query_intent: augmented reality overlay of real‑time pollutant concentrations on smartphone camera using ARCore or ARKit

orchestrator_plan.reason: AR 技术为空气质量数据提供沉浸式空间感知，是当前研究热点，未在已有策略中出现，具备高相关性和创新性。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 这个策略的目标是寻找能够支撑在智能手机摄像头上通过 ARCore 或 ARKit 实时叠加空气污染浓度的增强现实实现方案，期望获取与移动实验平台、实时传感、空间可视化或 AR‑化数据展示相关的技术与案例。然而，实际检索到的文献主要围绕传统的移动实验室在隧道内进行车辆尾气浓度测量、浓度梯度估算以及仪器响应时间校准的实验方法，此外还有热异常指数（TAI）在低温工业热源检测中的性能评估、VOC 采样现场图示以及站点与移动测量结果的对比图表。这些内容共同体现了“移动测量‑实时浓度获取‑多点数据可视化”这一主题，但并未出现任何与智能手机 AR 叠加、ARCore/ARKit 开发框架或基于摄像头的空间感知交互相关的直接线索。换言之，策略虽然成功捕获了与空气质量实时监测和可视化紧密相关的实验技术细节，却缺乏针对增强现实实现的具体方法或案例，提示后续需要更聚焦于 AR 平台、传感器‑手机接口或 AR 数据渲染的文献检索。

content.text: This study determined the usefulness and advantages of making mobile measurements of exhaust pollutants in a tunnel study. The safety issues and logistical difficulties in setting up analytical instrumentation inside a tunnel were overcome using a mobile laboratory. The heavy traffic in the urban tunnel did not enable sufficient room for the instrumentation and researchers to operate; thus, safety issues affected the operation of the research. Multipoint data generated by online instruments in a mobile laboratory can also help to overcome the various uncertainties associated with stationary measurements. This study identified a zonation inside the tunnel, i.e., mixing and accumulation zones, and recommended that the mixing zone be avoided to ensure accurate EF measurements. The concept of a concentration gradient was applied for EF calculations. The EF results from the mobile measurements were well matched with those from stationary measurements, while the absolute concentration differences between them suggested that further studies are required to assess the complex aerodynamic movements of the pollutants inside the tunnel. The proposed mobile measurement approach has potential for use in future tunnel studies. Because vehicle exhaust is recognized as one of the main sources of primary and secondary PM in large cities worldwide, there is an increasing need for more frequent and region-specific measurements of EFs in real environments for timely policymaking. Tunnel studies with mobile measurements can provide the required information.

content.text: This study determined the usefulness and advantages of making mobile measurements of exhaust pollutants in a tunnel study. The safety issues and logistical difficulties in setting up analytical instrumentation inside a tunnel were overcome using a mobile laboratory. The heavy traffic in the urban tunnel did not enable sufficient room for the instrumentation and researchers to operate; thus, safety issues affected the operation of the research. Multipoint data generated by online instruments in a mobile laboratory can also help to overcome the various uncertainties associated with stationary measurements. This study identified a zonation inside the tunnel, i.e., mixing and accumulation zones, and recommended that the mixing zone be avoided to ensure accurate EF measurements. The concept of a concentration gradient was applied for EF calculations. The EF results from the mobile measurements were well matched with those from stationary measurements, while the absolute concentration differences between them suggested that further studies are required to assess the complex aerodynamic movements of the pollutants inside the tunnel. The proposed mobile measurement approach has potential for use in future tunnel studies. Because vehicle exhaust is recognized as one of the main sources of primary and secondary PM in large cities worldwide, there is an increasing need for more frequent and region-specific measurements of EFs in real environments for timely policymaking. Tunnel studies with mobile measurements can provide the required information.

content.text: Confirmed with the VIIRS Nightfire product and high-spatial-resolution images, the TAI is definitely able to detect industrial heat sources with high confidence. Compared with the VIIRS Nightfire product, the TAI is more sensitive at low temperatures, allowing it to detect cooler or smaller hot spots. Nevertheless, the detection ability of the TAI is a little worse than that of the VIIRS Nightfire product at high temperatures. This feature confirms the low-temperature thermal detection ability of the TAI, and the inefficiency of TAI at extremely high temperatures does not weaken the detection of thermal anomalies. High water vapor and low background temperatures also improve the thermal detection ability of the TAI. In addition, we can use an empirical regression method to measure the FRP of the derived industrial heat sources, regardless of the difficulties for ASTER to obtain the areas and temperatures of hot spots. These characteristics make this method promising for urban environmental monitoring.

content.text: The figure shows photographs of a coating process in a workshop and the equipment used for sampling air to assess VOC emissions. The figure is used to illustrate the real-world context and methodology for collecting air samples to evaluate VOC exposure risks in industrial coating environments.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and heal", "figure_id": "img_214", "figure_title": "Fig. 1. Photos showing the (a) coating process and (b) sampling equipment.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and heal\\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Showcase Dataset Samples", "Introduce Experimental Setup"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Qualitative & Representational Imagery", "sub_types": ["Photograhp"]}, "key_entities": ["coating process", "sampling equipment", "furniture coating", "VOC emissions", "canister", "Entech Instrument"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows photographs of a coating process in a workshop and the equipment used for sampling air to assess VOC emissions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure is used to illustrate the real-world context and methodology for collecting air samples to evaluate VOC exposure risks in industrial coating environments.", "references": ["The description of the source samples is tabulated in Table 1. Three coating sectors in China were investigated in this study, namely cargo container coating, ship coating, and furniture coating. The coating processes were divided into primary coating, secondary coating, and tertiary coating, depending on the manufacturing flows in each industrial sector. In general, there was one worker in each coating workshop under normal working conditions. The workshops were  $60 - 150\\mathrm{m}^2$  for different coating processes in these industrial sectors. The central ventilation system was installed in the workplaces and the exhaust gas was discharged from the chimney and stack. The activated carbon-based masks were used by the workers. Whole-air samples were collected in the coating workshops that were being operated under normal conditions. Fig. 1(a) shows an example of the working condition of furniture coating.", "Indoor air was collected using 3.2-L fused silica stainless steel canisters that had been precleaned with high-purity nitrogen and evacuated with an automated canister cleaner. Each sample was taken for  $30\\mathrm{min}$  using an inlet with pre-calibrated flow regulator (CS1200 sampler; Entech Instrument, Inc.) operating at  $38~\\mathrm{mL~min}^{-1}$  (Entech Instruments Inc., California, USA), as shown in Fig. 1(b). The sampling equipment was held by the sampling staff at about  $1.5\\mathrm{-m}$  high, next to the occupational painter and less than  $1\\mathrm{m}$  away from the spraying source to represent a similar distance with the painter in real situations. Three samples were collected at similar position in each workshop to ensure the representative of the field samples. Canister vacuums were checked before and after sampling. The field samples and two blank samples were sent to the Peking University (PKU) VOCs laboratory for analysis immediately after sampling within one week of shipping time. The blank", "Solvent use and paint consumption are significant source sectors of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) emissions in China. The occupational painters have high risk of health effect due to exposure to high VOCs concentration. However, the toxic components in coating environment have not been carefully identified, and the health risks of VOCs exposure have not been sufficiently assessed. This study collected air samples from nine workshops of three major coating sectors in the Yangtze River Delta of China, namely cargo container coating, ship equipment coating, and furniture coating, to evaluate the noncancer and cancer risk of toxic VOCs exposure to occupational painters under a normal working condition. The results show that the container coating had highest cancer risk  $(2.29 \\times 10^{-6} - 5.53 \\times 10^{-6})$  exceeding the safe limit of  $1.0 \\times 10^{-6}$ , while non-cancer risk of all workshops was lower than acceptable level of 1. Ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane should be targeted for priority removal during the container coating process in attempt to reduce adverse health effect on the occupational painters. This study helps better understand the health risk of VOCs exposure in coating workshops in China and provides information for policy-makers to formulate possible control of specific toxic compounds during coating process."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: It is important to align the time lag between the air sample intake from the inlet and the response time of each instrument during mobile measurements (Perkins et al., 2013). Response time differences can be caused by differences in the length of the sampling tubing and analysis time of each instrument. To calibrate the response time differences, the time lag for each instrument was determined using  $\mathrm{N}_2$  gas at the inlets of each instrument. The lag times for the DustTrak, SIFT-MS, gas analyzers, and CRDS were 0, 30, 60, and  $120\mathrm{s}$ , respectively. All instruments were powered by a generator mounted inside a mobile laboratory. The air sample inlet was installed on the rooftop of the mobile laboratory, approximately  $3\mathrm{m}$  above the ground level. This placement helped to avoid direct exposure to exhaust emissions from vehicles in front. To further minimize any potential impact of high emitters, we carefully reviewed the raw data and dashcam footage. However, we did not observe any transient spikes during the measurements. Additionally, we derived concentration profiles by averaging data from 12 transect profiles. This approach helped to mitigate any direct effects of emissions from high emitters. The inhaled air sample was distributed to each instrument through a manifold system using a 1/4-inch polytetrafluoroethylene tube. The traveling speed of the mobile laboratory during monitoring was maintained at less than  $40\mathrm{km}\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ .

orchestrator_plan.reason: 利用降维技术对多污染物数据进行可视化，可帮助揭示源解析模式，提供一种全新的数据探索视角，符合高相关性和新颖性要求。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 这个检索策略的目标是寻找能够用降维技术（t‑SNE、UMAP 等）对多污染物数据进行可视化、进而实现来源归属分析的方法和实例。实际得到的十条结果大多是传统的可视化形式——条形图、箱线图、网格地图、垂直剖面图或环形柱状图——用于展示 AQI、PM2.5、SO₂、NO₂ 的区域分布、不同收入组的排放水平、亚洲的排放网格、道路两侧的粒子垂直分布以及 VOC 对臭氧和二次有机气溶胶的贡献等；还有一篇强调深度学习在空气质量预测中的作用以及一项利用空间自相关分析的区域时空变化研究。这些内容共同体现了“空气污染数据的可视化”这一宏观主题，但均未涉及使用 t‑SNE、UMAP 等多变量降维手段进行源解析的案例。尽管如此，结果中提供的高分辨率排放网格、VOC 贡献的定量图形以及空间自相关分析的工具（ArcGIS、GeoDa）等，仍然能为后续构建降维可视化管线提供有价值的输入数据和参考线索。

content.text: The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions. The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden", "figure_id": "img_71", "figure_title": "Fig. 4. Estimated coefficients (date) prior to and following the start time of heating.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\\figure_4.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "PM2.5", "SO2", "NO2", "Eastern region", "Western region", "WCHP project"], "concise_summary": "The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.", "references": ["As Fig. 4 shows, the 15 day estimator prior to and following the start of heating varied around a value of zero (0) devoid of a clear trend, and most coefficients were not statistically significant. This result indicated a similarity between pilot cities and non-pilot cities regarding AQI. Despite an abnormal increase 4-6 days following the start time of heating, such differences decreased to a negative value (as seen 2 days prior to the start time of heating), and this trend maintained for the ensuing two weeks. These results were consistent with the hypothesis that the WCHP project generated differential trends in pollutant concentrations between pilot and non-pilot cities. It is noteworthy that the period prior to the start time of heating was implemented and continued for 3-4 days in some cities prior to the formal start time of heating, which may have resulted in the early decrease. Additionally, we also estimated the Heatperiod coefficient 30 days following the start time of heating, which also indicated that effects of the WCHP project existed following the start time of heating.", "where  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  is the set of dummy variables, which represents whether the  $i$ -th city is in a heating period at  $n-1$ -day.  $L_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days prior to the start time of heating, and  $E_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days following the start time of heating. With the omitted time category being the start time of heating,  $L_n$  captures the differences in pollutant indicators between pilot and non-pilot cities relative to differences just prior to the launch of the policy. Here we selected AQI as the air pollutant proxy. Under such specifications, if the signs of the estimated  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  coefficients differ, it can be assumed that changes in pollutant concentrations are not significant.", "As a second robustness check, we constructed a counterfactual test to check whether our results could be driven by other unobserved factors. In doing so, we selected a different date during the non-heating period (May, June, July, August, September, and October) in lieu of the real start date of heating, specifying the interaction term between the treatment variable and the pseudoheating start time variable as Pseudo-HCP. The WCHP project can only take effect during the heating period, while the pseudo-HCP coefficient can reflect the impact of the WCHP project during the non-heating period. If estimated coefficients of pseudo-WCHP are significant and have the same sign as of the true WCHP shown in Table 4, we can conclude that air quality improvements in pilot cities could not be mainly attributed to the WCHP project, and the robustness of the DID model would be inadequate. In this case, improvements may be related to other pollution control policies, such as production restrictions and limits to the number of private vehicle allowed on roads.", "According to the regressions shown in Table 5, only when the start time of heating was specified in October, coefficients of pseudo-WCHP were significantly negative at a  $1\\%$  level, while other estimation results were not significant or had a positive sign. Compared to Table 4, these conflicting results failed to reject the validity of the \"WHCD\" policy. That is to say, the effect of the WCHP project was evident. In fact, we specified the heating start time on the 15th of November in consideration of the \"official\" start time of central heating while ignoring differences caused by scattered coal combustion. In October, some rural areas in the pilot and non-pilot cities began to burn scattered coal for heating, which could cause an evident increase in pollution emissions [47,48]."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: Compared with traditional techniques for forecasting air pollution, namely empirical approaches and simple statistical models, deep learning approaches can handle complex and non-linear relationships among air quality variables which allow them to better capture the pollution episode forecasts. Therefore, deep learning has been identified as a vital tool for air quality modelling and forecasting, which helps in recognizing pollution hot spots (Schmidhuber, 2015; Ong et al., 2016; Li et al., 2017).

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

metadata.metadata:
{"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_165", "figure_title": "Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "Guan, D., Liu, Z., Geng, Y., Lindner, S., and Hubacek, K.: The gigatonne gap in China's carbon dioxide inventories, Nature Clim. content.text: Vertical profiles show particle concentrations upwind and downwind of a motorway, revealing height-dependent differences and correlations with NOx levels. The figure illustrates how particle concentrations vary with height and time, confirming correlations with NOx and revealing impacts of emissions on air quality.

Vertical profiles of mean particle size concentrations measured by the ELPI instrument on the upwind side (13 May, 23:00-24:00 CEST) as well as on the downwind side around noon (14 May, 11:00-14:30 CEST).", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Comparison of measured and model-calculated real-w\\figure_5.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Heatmap"]}, "key_entities": ["ELPI instrument", "particle size concentrations", "upwind side", "downwind side", "plume", "motorway", "soot particles", "nucleation mode particles", "tyre and brake abrasion", "resuspension", "particle number concentrations", "NOx concentrations", "emission factors", "LDV", "HDV"], "concise_summary": "Vertical profiles show particle concentrations upwind and downwind of a motorway, revealing height-dependent differences and correlations with NOx levels.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates how particle concentrations vary with height and time, confirming correlations with NOx and revealing impacts of emissions on air quality.", "references": ["For the first time, vertical profiles with high temporal and spatial resolution of particulate air pollutants could be recorded by tower elevators (Imhof et al., 2005). Upwind the motorway, no height-dependent difference in the particle number and surface area concentration was observed. On the downwind side, substantially higher concentrations were measured within the plume from near the ground up to  $20\\mathrm{m}$  height than above the plume (Fig. 5). This height dependency was found for particles with diameter  $< 300\\mathrm{nm}$ , which are soot particles and nucleation mode particles formed by condensation of exhaust gases in most cases and for coarse particles (diameter  $>1\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ), originating from tyre and brake abrasion and resuspension of soil dust. Between 300 and  $700\\mathrm{nm}$  particle size, no height dependence was found, because motor vehicles emit only a few particles in this size range.", "After adjusting the instruments for their different time lags, the vertical distribution of the particle number concentrations showed a similar behaviour as the  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  concentrations: No height dependence on the upwind side and a continuous decrease with increasing height from 5 to  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above the earth's surface on the downwind side was found. Consequently, the results in this study confirmed the correlation between particles and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  values that had also been observed in former case studies. A linear relation between the particle surface area (SA) and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  with correlation coefficients of  $R^2 = 0.96$  and 0.89 for 2 downwind cases was found. At around  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above ground, the background value of SA was attained. By subtracting the background value from the concentrations measured in the near-surface air layer, direct emissions of the road were obtained, even if contemporaneous upwind and downwind measurements were missing. Together with the parallel wind measurements, mean absolute emission factors (EF) were determined. The PM emissions in the range between  $30\\mathrm{nm}$  and  $10\\mu \\mathrm{m}$  from all vehicles passing the site were found to be  $1.80\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ . Separation into the categories of LDV led to  $1.22\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.03\\mathrm{cm}^3\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  aerosol volume, while the values of HDV were  $7.79\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.41\\mathrm{cm}^3\\mathrm{km}^{-1}$  veh $^{-1}$  particle volume.", "Fig. 4. Vertical profiles of mean particle size distributions measured by SMPS instruments at three different heights on the upwind side of the motorway (13 May 2001, midnight) and on the downwind side (14 May 2001, morning rush hour and noon)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: To further analyze the temporal and spatial variation features of air quality in 31 provincial capital cities from 2013 to 2020, the spatial autocorrelation analysis method is applied. ArcGIS 10.6 and GeoDa software is used for the calculation and analytic process.
